1	# Fable recommendation — rbfl_jettison success message claims a tolerance the code refuses
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-10
4	Status: recommendation from the post-wrap ₣BH review (second Fable pass); not yet acted on.
5	
6	## The behavior
7	
8	`rbfl_jettison` (rbfld_Delete.sh) ends with `buc_success "Jettisoned or nonexistent: <ref>"`,
9	but the code accepts only HTTP 202/204 and `buc_die`s on everything else — including the 404 a
10	nonexistent ref returns from the Docker v2 manifests DELETE. The message describes an idempotent
11	delete; the code implements a strict one. One of them is wrong.
12	
13	## Recommended repair
14	
15	Tolerate 404 as success — i.e., make the code match the message, not the reverse.
16	Idempotent deletes are the house philosophy (`rbuh_poll_until_gone`, the rbgjl06 convergence loop's
17	404-is-already-gone), and a cleanup-of-last-resort verb that dies on already-gone is the wrong
18	shape for its job. If strictness is preferred instead, change the message to "Jettisoned" and let
19	404 keep dying loud.
20	
21	## Adjacent note for the record (no action)
22	
23	The ₢BHAAa docket cinched "honest LRO handling (`rbge_lro_ok` where GAR returns an LRO)"; the
24	landed verb instead rides the Docker v2 manifests DELETE, where no LRO exists, trusting 202/204.
25	Live verification confirmed the 202 corresponds to actual deletion, and a missed delete is visible
26	via `rbw-il`, so this is acceptable — but it is a trust-202 cousin of the gap this heat closed for
27	package-grain deletes, and worth remembering if jettison ever misbehaves.
28

file.content:
# Fable recommendation — rbfl_jettison success message claims a tolerance the code refuses

Date: 2026-06-10
Status: recommendation from the post-wrap ₣BH review (second Fable pass); not yet acted on.

## The behavior

`rbfl_jettison` (rbfld_Delete.sh) ends with `buc_success "Jettisoned or nonexistent: <ref>"`,
but the code accepts only HTTP 202/204 and `buc_die`s on everything else — including the 404 a
nonexistent ref returns from the Docker v2 manifests DELETE. The message describes an idempotent
delete; the code implements a strict one. One of them is wrong.

## Recommended repair

Tolerate 404 as success — i.e., make the code match the message, not the reverse.
Idempotent deletes are the house philosophy (`rbuh_poll_until_gone`, the rbgjl06 convergence loop's
404-is-already-gone), and a cleanup-of-last-resort verb that dies on already-gone is the wrong
shape for its job. If strictness is preferred instead, change the message to "Jettisoned" and let
404 keep dying loud.

## Adjacent note for the record (no action)

The ₢BHAAa docket cinched "honest LRO handling (`rbge_lro_ok` where GAR returns an LRO)"; the
landed verb instead rides the Docker v2 manifests DELETE, where no LRO exists, trusting 202/204.
Live verification confirmed the 202 corresponds to actual deletion, and a missed delete is visible
via `rbw-il`, so this is acceptable — but it is a trust-202 cousin of the gap this heat closed for
package-grain deletes, and worth remembering if jettison ever misbehaves.

# ₣BH Fable Recommendation — Pin the Delete Builder by Digest

Date: 2026-06-10

Status: Recommendation from the Fable review of the cloud-dispatch delete architecture
(commit 4f8a5c703). Not a blocker for the landed pace; priority hardening.

## The correctable behavior

`ZRBFC_DELETE_BUILDER="gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud:latest"` — minted in `zrbfc_kindle`
(`Tools/rbk/rbfc0_FoundryCore.sh`) — is a floating, unpinned builder image, and the delete
build runs **as the Director SA** (`zrbld_cloud_delete_dispatch`, `Tools/rbk/rbldd_Delete.sh`),
the most GAR-privileged identity in the system (`repoAdmin`, delete over the whole repository).

Every prior floating-builder use (underpin/wsl gcrane bootstrap) runs as **Mason, writer-only**
— that asymmetry was the design: the identity that executes unpinned or untrusted bytes never
holds delete authority. The delete build breaks the asymmetry: a poisoned `:latest` executes
with delete authority, and the in-pool metadata server hands it Director access tokens
(`metadata_token()` in `Tools/rbk/rbgjl/rbgjl06-package-delete.py` demonstrates the fetch).

The comment on the constant already cites the bootstrap-pin itch (RBS0 `rbsk_pinning_boundary`)
as accepted. The Director-identity pairing escalates that itch from hygiene to priority **for
this builder specifically**.

## Recommended repair

Pin `ZRBFC_DELETE_BUILDER` by digest:
`gcr.io/cloud-builders/gcloud@sha256:<digest>`, resolved once by hand (or by a small
`gcrane digest` gesture) and recorded in the constant with a dated comment.

- Preserves the reliquary-less property the delete build requires (a digest pin needs no
  reliquary; the delete of the last reliquary still works).
- The step's needs are frozen (python3 + urllib + json), so staleness cost is near zero;
  refresh the digest deliberately, as a reviewed one-line change, rather than riding `:latest`.
- Update the RBSCB "Cloud-Dispatched Tool-Plane Deletes" posture section to record the pin
  when done (it currently inherits the floating-bootstrap framing).

1	# Fable recommendation — python cloud steps need an import allowlist (and a subprocess policy)
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-10
4	Status: recommendation, grounded in code (second Fable review pass); not yet acted on. Operator-prompted:
5	"should we create an equivalent of the bash command allowlist for python, anchored on imports?"
6	
7	## Verdict: yes — and the gap is already carrying live traffic
8	
9	The cupel's supply-chain conformance walks `*.sh` only (`zrbtdru_walk_sh`,
10	`ZRBTDRU_UNIVERSE_FILE_EXT` in rbtdru_cupel.rs). Four python cloud steps exist today —
11	`rbgja01-discover-platforms.py`, `rbgja03-build-info-per-platform.py`,
12	`rbgjl06-package-delete.py`, `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` — and sit entirely outside the
13	conformance surface. A `.py` step sidesteps the bash-command discipline by construction.
14	
15	The live specimen proving the hole is not hypothetical: **rbgjv02 `subprocess.run`s `gcloud`** —
16	a command absent from `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED` — invisibly to the cupel. The invocation itself may be
17	legitimate (the SLSA provenance `describe`), but it is *unreviewed by the mechanism*, which is the
18	whole point of having one. CBG already names the rule this would mechanize (CBi_104 "code to the
19	builder image's pinned runtime") and already has the `CBp_` python family with a known-gap marker
20	(CBp_101) — the import allowlist is the missing citer.
21	
22	## Recommended shape
23	
24	Extend the cupel with a `*.py` walk over the step directories and two checks:
25	
26	1. **Import allowlist, anchored on the module root** of every `import X` / `from X import Y`.
27	   Current empirical floor (the union of all four steps): `base64, datetime, io, json, os, sys,
28	   tarfile, time, urllib`. Everything else flagged. `importlib`, `__import__`, `exec`, `eval`
29	   banned outright — dynamic import defeats static conformance. Third-party imports (`requests`
30	   et al.) are the real quarry: they bind a step to the floating builder's unpinned pip set, the
31	   exact drift class the bash allowlist exists to stop. Stdlib-only is the right floor.
32	
33	2. **Subprocess policy — the bridge between the two universes.** `subprocess` lets a python step
34	   shell out past both disciplines. Either ban it in steps (python steps exist for in-process
35	   JSON/HTTP work; shelling out belongs in bash steps), or — since rbgjv02 uses it today — scan
36	   `subprocess.run([...])` argv[0] literals against the same `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED` list: one tool
37	   floor, two languages. Under that option, `gcloud` needs explicit adjudication (add it to the
38	   allowlist, or convert rbgjv02's describe call to REST urllib like every other step).
39	
40	## Interaction with the standing builder-pin memo
41	
42	memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-pin-delete-builder flags the floating builder under a
43	privileged identity; this memo is its step-body complement. Floating builder × unscanned imports
44	compound: the builder decides what `import requests` resolves to. Pinning bounds the supply;
45	the allowlist bounds the demand.
46	
47	## Addenda from the 2026-06-10 CBG exchange (operator review of these recommendations)
48	
49	- **Where the authoritative tool floor lives (surveyed):** the doc comment on
50	  `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED` in `rbtdru_cupel.rs` is the single clearest statement — it carries the
51	  membership criterion (empirical per-container presence in the controlled builders, NOT kit
52	  portability), the jq-exclusion rationale, and the conformance purpose. No spec or guide copy
53	  exists, and none should be created: CBG should carry a **pointer** to that constant as the floor's
54	  home ("reference the home, don't recreate" — ACG), never a restated list. CBG's CBi_105/CBp_102
55	  currently reference the concept; the pointer-line addition awaits operator placement choice.
56	- **Why the unsanctioned `gcloud` never failed an audit (operator question):** vacuous pass — the
57	  cupel never opens `.py` files, so rbgjv02 was never scanned at all. The audit did not tolerate the
58	  violation; it never saw it. No additional mechanism is implicated beyond the gap this memo records.
59	- **Probe before building the python walk:** check whether `zrbfc_expand_includes`
60	  (rbfcb_BuildHost.sh) is already language-blind — the `#@rbgjs_include` marker is a `#` comment,
61	  valid python syntax, so a shared python preamble snippet (`die`/`require_env`/`metadata_token`/
62	  `gar_fetch`/`gar_json`) might splice with zero expander work, resolving CBG CBp_101 (the
63	  preamble-duplication gap) nearly for free. Ten-minute probe; do it when planning the walk pace —
64	  the shebang-strip and any bash-shaped assumptions in the expander are the things to check.
65	- **buildStepOutputs cap is API contract, not folklore:** the Build API's `results.buildStepOutputs`
66	  stores "only the first 4KB" of `$BUILDER_OUTPUT/output`, ordered by build step index
67	  (https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema; the cloudbuild v1 API model docs
68	  state the 4KB retention on BuildStep outputs). Now cited in CBG CBh_103. Relevant ceiling for the
69	  podvm native-family 8-leaf envelope.
70

# Fable recommendation — python cloud steps need an import allowlist (and a subprocess policy)

Date: 2026-06-10
Status: recommendation, grounded in code (second Fable review pass); not yet acted on. Operator-prompted:
"should we create an equivalent of the bash command allowlist for python, anchored on imports?"

## Verdict: yes — and the gap is already carrying live traffic

The cupel's supply-chain conformance walks `*.sh` only (`zrbtdru_walk_sh`,
`ZRBTDRU_UNIVERSE_FILE_EXT` in rbtdru_cupel.rs). Four python cloud steps exist today —
`rbgja01-discover-platforms.py`, `rbgja03-build-info-per-platform.py`,
`rbgjl06-package-delete.py`, `rbgjv02-verify-provenance.py` — and sit entirely outside the
conformance surface. A `.py` step sidesteps the bash-command discipline by construction.

The live specimen proving the hole is not hypothetical: **rbgjv02 `subprocess.run`s `gcloud`** —
a command absent from `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED` — invisibly to the cupel. The invocation itself may be
legitimate (the SLSA provenance `describe`), but it is *unreviewed by the mechanism*, which is the
whole point of having one. CBG already names the rule this would mechanize (CBi_104 "code to the
builder image's pinned runtime") and already has the `CBp_` python family with a known-gap marker
(CBp_101) — the import allowlist is the missing citer.

## Recommended shape

Extend the cupel with a `*.py` walk over the step directories and two checks:

1. **Import allowlist, anchored on the module root** of every `import X` / `from X import Y`.
   Current empirical floor (the union of all four steps): `base64, datetime, io, json, os, sys,
   tarfile, time, urllib`. Everything else flagged. `importlib`, `__import__`, `exec`, `eval`
   banned outright — dynamic import defeats static conformance. Third-party imports (`requests`
   et al.) are the real quarry: they bind a step to the floating builder's unpinned pip set, the
   exact drift class the bash allowlist exists to stop. Stdlib-only is the right floor.

2. **Subprocess policy — the bridge between the two universes.** `subprocess` lets a python step
   shell out past both disciplines. Either ban it in steps (python steps exist for in-process
   JSON/HTTP work; shelling out belongs in bash steps), or — since rbgjv02 uses it today — scan
   `subprocess.run([...])` argv[0] literals against the same `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED` list: one tool
   floor, two languages. Under that option, `gcloud` needs explicit adjudication (add it to the
   allowlist, or convert rbgjv02's describe call to REST urllib like every other step).

## Interaction with the standing builder-pin memo

memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-pin-delete-builder flags the floating builder under a
privileged identity; this memo is its step-body complement. Floating builder × unscanned imports
compound: the builder decides what `import requests` resolves to. Pinning bounds the supply;
the allowlist bounds the demand.

## Addenda from the 2026-06-10 CBG exchange (operator review of these recommendations)

- **Where the authoritative tool floor lives (surveyed):** the doc comment on
  `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED` in `rbtdru_cupel.rs` is the single clearest statement — it carries the
  membership criterion (empirical per-container presence in the controlled builders, NOT kit
  portability), the jq-exclusion rationale, and the conformance purpose. No spec or guide copy
  exists, and none should be created: CBG should carry a **pointer** to that constant as the floor's
  home ("reference the home, don't recreate" — ACG), never a restated list. CBG's CBi_105/CBp_102
  currently reference the concept; the pointer-line addition awaits operator placement choice.
- **Why the unsanctioned `gcloud` never failed an audit (operator question):** vacuous pass — the
  cupel never opens `.py` files, so rbgjv02 was never scanned at all. The audit did not tolerate the
  violation; it never saw it. No additional mechanism is implicated beyond the gap this memo records.
- **Probe before building the python walk:** check whether `zrbfc_expand_includes`
  (rbfcb_BuildHost.sh) is already language-blind — the `#@rbgjs_include` marker is a `#` comment,
  valid python syntax, so a shared python preamble snippet (`die`/`require_env`/`metadata_token`/
  `gar_fetch`/`gar_json`) might splice with zero expander work, resolving CBG CBp_101 (the
  preamble-duplication gap) nearly for free. Ten-minute probe; do it when planning the walk pace —
  the shebang-strip and any bash-shaped assumptions in the expander are the things to check.
- **buildStepOutputs cap is API contract, not folklore:** the Build API's `results.buildStepOutputs`
  stores "only the first 4KB" of `$BUILDER_OUTPUT/output`, ordered by build step index
  (https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema; the cloudbuild v1 API model docs
  state the 4KB retention on BuildStep outputs). Now cited in CBG CBh_103. Relevant ceiling for the
  podvm native-family 8-leaf envelope.

content.content:
1	# ₣BH Fable Recommendation — Tolerate URLError in fire_delete
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-10
4	
5	Status: Recommendation from the Fable review of the cloud-dispatch delete architecture
6	(commit 4f8a5c703). Small consistency repair in the convergence step.
7	
8	## The correctable behavior
9	
10	`fire_delete` (`Tools/rbk/rbgjl/rbgjl06-package-delete.py`) catches only
11	`urllib.error.HTTPError`. A transient **`urllib.error.URLError`** — connection reset, DNS
12	blip, broken pipe — propagates uncaught, crashes the step with a traceback, and fails the
13	whole build.
14	
15	That is inconsistent with the step's own philosophy: a TCP reset is morally the same event as
16	a 503, and the design explicitly declares that no single call's verdict matters — "absence is
17	the only truth; per-call delete errors are logged for the build trail, never branched on."
18	The convergence loop exists to absorb exactly this class of transient; the catch clause
19	absorbs only half of it.
20	
21	## Recommended repair
22	
23	In `fire_delete`, catch `urllib.error.URLError` alongside `HTTPError` (note `HTTPError` is a
24	subclass of `URLError`, so order or structure the handler accordingly), log it in the same
25	`(reconciling via absence poll)` form, and continue. The next round retries; the deadline
26	remains the failsafe.
27	
28	**Deliberately do not extend this tolerance to the truth-readers**: `package_absent` and
29	`list_version_ids` should stay fail-fast (die loud on anything unexpected), because they are
30	the arbiters the convergence rests on — a flaky reader silently misreporting absence is the
31	one failure the design must never absorb. If their fail-fast proves noisy under real network
32	weather, a single bounded retry is the most they should gain.
33

file.content:
# ₣BH Fable Recommendation — Tolerate URLError in fire_delete

Date: 2026-06-10

Status: Recommendation from the Fable review of the cloud-dispatch delete architecture
(commit 4f8a5c703). Small consistency repair in the convergence step.

## The correctable behavior

`fire_delete` (`Tools/rbk/rbgjl/rbgjl06-package-delete.py`) catches only
`urllib.error.HTTPError`. A transient **`urllib.error.URLError`** — connection reset, DNS
blip, broken pipe — propagates uncaught, crashes the step with a traceback, and fails the
whole build.

That is inconsistent with the step's own philosophy: a TCP reset is morally the same event as
a 503, and the design explicitly declares that no single call's verdict matters — "absence is
the only truth; per-call delete errors are logged for the build trail, never branched on."
The convergence loop exists to absorb exactly this class of transient; the catch clause
absorbs only half of it.

## Recommended repair

In `fire_delete`, catch `urllib.error.URLError` alongside `HTTPError` (note `HTTPError` is a
subclass of `URLError`, so order or structure the handler accordingly), log it in the same
`(reconciling via absence poll)` form, and continue. The next round retries; the deadline
remains the failsafe.

**Deliberately do not extend this tolerance to the truth-readers**: `package_absent` and
`list_version_ids` should stay fail-fast (die loud on anything unexpected), because they are
the arbiters the convergence rests on — a flaky reader silently misreporting absence is the
one failure the design must never absorb. If their fail-fast proves noisy under real network
weather, a single bounded retry is the most they should gain.

content.content:
1	# ₣BH Fable Recommendation — Bound urllib Calls with a Socket Timeout
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-10
4	
5	Status: Recommendation from the Fable review of the cloud-dispatch delete architecture
6	(commit 4f8a5c703). Cheap hang-bounding in the convergence step.
7	
8	## The correctable behavior
9	
10	Every `urllib.request.urlopen` call in `Tools/rbk/rbgjl/rbgjl06-package-delete.py` —
11	`metadata_token()` and `gar_fetch()` (which carries `package_absent`, `list_version_ids`,
12	and `fire_delete`) — passes **no timeout**. Python's default is `None`: an unbounded block
13	on a hung socket.
14	
15	The failsafes that eventually fire are the wrong grain: a single hung GET stalls the entire
16	delete until the Cloud Build timeout (`RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT=2700s`) or the host poll ceiling
17	kills the build — turning one stuck connection into a full-build failure with no log line
18	naming the stall.
19	
20	## Recommended repair
21	
22	Pass an explicit timeout to `urlopen` in `gar_fetch` and `metadata_token` — 30s is generous
23	for both the metadata server and GAR REST.
24	
25	Classify the resulting `socket.timeout` / `TimeoutError` by caller, consistent with the
26	URLError-tolerance recommendation (companion memo):
27	
28	- In `fire_delete`: tolerated and logged like any other per-call error — the absence poll
29	  arbitrates.
30	- In the truth-readers (`package_absent`, `list_version_ids`) and `metadata_token`: die loud
31	  (or one bounded retry) — a timed-out read names its stall in the build log instead of
32	  freezing silently to the build timeout.
33

file.content:
# ₣BH Fable Recommendation — Bound urllib Calls with a Socket Timeout

Date: 2026-06-10

Status: Recommendation from the Fable review of the cloud-dispatch delete architecture
(commit 4f8a5c703). Cheap hang-bounding in the convergence step.

## The correctable behavior

Every `urllib.request.urlopen` call in `Tools/rbk/rbgjl/rbgjl06-package-delete.py` —
`metadata_token()` and `gar_fetch()` (which carries `package_absent`, `list_version_ids`,
and `fire_delete`) — passes **no timeout**. Python's default is `None`: an unbounded block
on a hung socket.

The failsafes that eventually fire are the wrong grain: a single hung GET stalls the entire
delete until the Cloud Build timeout (`RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT=2700s`) or the host poll ceiling
kills the build — turning one stuck connection into a full-build failure with no log line
naming the stall.

## Recommended repair

Pass an explicit timeout to `urlopen` in `gar_fetch` and `metadata_token` — 30s is generous
for both the metadata server and GAR REST.

Classify the resulting `socket.timeout` / `TimeoutError` by caller, consistent with the
URLError-tolerance recommendation (companion memo):

- In `fire_delete`: tolerated and logged like any other per-call error — the absence poll
  arbitrates.
- In the truth-readers (`package_absent`, `list_version_ids`) and `metadata_token`: die loud
  (or one bounded retry) — a timed-out read names its stall in the build log instead of
  freezing silently to the build timeout.

1	# Fable review — ₢BHAAW immure pace, noncritical findings + carried follow-ups
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-10
4	Status: noncritical findings from the pre-wrap adversarial review of the podvm immure pace.
5	Nothing here blocks the wrap; the two stale-comment sites that DID block it went to the
6	implementing session directly. All items below are triage material for the ₣BH terminal
7	memo-walk pace.
8	
9	## Findings (Fable, this review)
10	
11	1. **rbgjl07 selection-entry fields are not asserted non-empty.** An entry like `:aarch64`
12	   (empty disktype) passes the `":" in entry` check and then *matches any descriptor lacking a
13	   disktype annotation* (`ann.get("disktype","") == ""`). Selection is a curated constant, not
14	   user input, so exposure is low — but one `test`-shaped assert (both fields non-empty) makes
15	   the malformed-constant failure loud instead of weird.
16	2. **rbgjl08/09 run network tools inside `while read < file` loops.** The house-documented
17	   stdin hazard (see rbld_divine's load-then-iterate comment: a child touching stdin consumes
18	   the loop's remaining input). gcrane/curl don't read stdin today and the live 2-member run
19	   processed both rows — but busybox sh has no arrays, so the cheap belt is `< /dev/null` on the
20	   in-loop tool calls rather than load-then-iterate.
21	3. **ALLOW_LOOSE also forfeits dead-key detection.** The spine comment correctly says the
22	   dispatch-time scan replaces MUST_MATCH's requires-side check; the provides-side check (a blob
23	   key no step references) is now unguarded in both layers. Harmless — a dead key costs nothing —
24	   recorded so nobody rediscovers it as a surprise.
25	4. **The CBp_102 python import floor accreted within a day** (`re`, via rbgjl07). CBG updated to
26	   include it and to state the enumeration is the temporary home. Evidence for the cupel python
27	   walk owning the floor mechanically (memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-python-import-allowlist).
28	5. **Two more Director-RBRA manifold sites landed** (`rbldv_Immure.sh` sources the RBRA file and
29	   passes the path to the token mint, copied from the rbldb template). Consistent with existing
30	   code — counted here only so the ₣BZ accessor-seam sweep knows its census grew.
31	
32	## Carried follow-ups (flagged by the implementing session at wrap-readiness)
33	
34	6. **rbw-di egress-check inventory**: add a line for quay.io's blob-CDN redirect hosts — the
35	   first breakage point if worker-pool egress posture ever tightens.
36	7. **buildStepOutputs 4KB ceiling vs the native family**: the 8-leaf podvm-native envelope
37	   should be size-checked against the now-cited 4KB contract (CBG CBh_103) before the FOLLOWING
38	   pace reuses the envelope-in-slot shape. Cheap arithmetic at design time, expensive surprise
39	   at runtime.
40	8. **The RBSL spec family (RBSLA/B/C/D/E/I/U) is not catalogued in claude-rbk-acronyms.md** —
41	   family-wide tidy, one entry block.
42

# Fable review — ₢BHAAW immure pace, noncritical findings + carried follow-ups

Date: 2026-06-10
Status: noncritical findings from the pre-wrap adversarial review of the podvm immure pace.
Nothing here blocks the wrap; the two stale-comment sites that DID block it went to the
implementing session directly. All items below are triage material for the ₣BH terminal
memo-walk pace.

## Findings (Fable, this review)

1. **rbgjl07 selection-entry fields are not asserted non-empty.** An entry like `:aarch64`
   (empty disktype) passes the `":" in entry` check and then *matches any descriptor lacking a
   disktype annotation* (`ann.get("disktype","") == ""`). Selection is a curated constant, not
   user input, so exposure is low — but one `test`-shaped assert (both fields non-empty) makes
   the malformed-constant failure loud instead of weird.
2. **rbgjl08/09 run network tools inside `while read < file` loops.** The house-documented
   stdin hazard (see rbld_divine's load-then-iterate comment: a child touching stdin consumes
   the loop's remaining input). gcrane/curl don't read stdin today and the live 2-member run
   processed both rows — but busybox sh has no arrays, so the cheap belt is `< /dev/null` on the
   in-loop tool calls rather than load-then-iterate.
3. **ALLOW_LOOSE also forfeits dead-key detection.** The spine comment correctly says the
   dispatch-time scan replaces MUST_MATCH's requires-side check; the provides-side check (a blob
   key no step references) is now unguarded in both layers. Harmless — a dead key costs nothing —
   recorded so nobody rediscovers it as a surprise.
4. **The CBp_102 python import floor accreted within a day** (`re`, via rbgjl07). CBG updated to
   include it and to state the enumeration is the temporary home. Evidence for the cupel python
   walk owning the floor mechanically (memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-python-import-allowlist).
5. **Two more Director-RBRA manifold sites landed** (`rbldv_Immure.sh` sources the RBRA file and
   passes the path to the token mint, copied from the rbldb template). Consistent with existing
   code — counted here only so the ₣BZ accessor-seam sweep knows its census grew.

## Carried follow-ups (flagged by the implementing session at wrap-readiness)

6. **rbw-di egress-check inventory**: add a line for quay.io's blob-CDN redirect hosts — the
   first breakage point if worker-pool egress posture ever tightens.
7. **buildStepOutputs 4KB ceiling vs the native family**: the 8-leaf podvm-native envelope
   should be size-checked against the now-cited 4KB contract (CBG CBh_103) before the FOLLOWING
   pace reuses the envelope-in-slot shape. Cheap arithmetic at design time, expensive surprise
   at runtime.
8. **The RBSL spec family (RBSLA/B/C/D/E/I/U) is not catalogued in claude-rbk-acronyms.md** —
   family-wide tidy, one entry block.

content.content:
1	# Fast-tier credlessness is convention, not construction — near-miss record
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-10
4	Status: standing-hazard record from the podvm refresh pace; the immediate instance was caught
5	pre-run and fixed with a buo test-seam. Triage material for the ₣BH terminal memo-walk pace.
6	
7	## The near-miss
8	
9	The fast-tier podvm resolve case invokes the real `rbw-lI` tabtarget against the real working
10	regime — theurge isolates only BURV temp/output dirs, never credentials. On a credentialed
11	workstation (the operator's always is), the case would have sailed past its asserted diagnostic
12	into a live multi-GB Cloud Build submission, inside the `fast` suite, on every run.
13	Caught by inspection before any run; fixed by short-circuiting the verb under a
14	`buorb_immure_resolve_only` tweak.
15	
16	## The standing hazard
17	
18	Nothing structural makes the fast tier credless. Every existing fast fixture is safe only
19	because none happened to invoke a GCP-capable colophon — the first one that did nearly fired a
20	live build. Future fast cases can repeat this silently: the failure mode is a *passing* fast
21	suite that spends money and mutates the depot.
22	
23	## Triage question
24	
25	Whether to enforce fast-tier credlessness by construction. Candidate shapes (not designed, just
26	recorded): the fast suite runner exports a poison marker that the credential-load path refuses
27	(loud "fast tier reached a credential load" die); or theurge's fast tier asserts no network
28	egress; or a per-case declaration ("reaches GCP: yes/no") validated against the tier. Each has
29	costs; the buo-seam-per-case convention may also be judged sufficient — but then the convention
30	needs stating somewhere an author of a new fast case will read it.
31

# Fast-tier credlessness is convention, not construction — near-miss record

Date: 2026-06-10
Status: standing-hazard record from the podvm refresh pace; the immediate instance was caught
pre-run and fixed with a buo test-seam. Triage material for the ₣BH terminal memo-walk pace.

## The near-miss

The fast-tier podvm resolve case invokes the real `rbw-lI` tabtarget against the real working
regime — theurge isolates only BURV temp/output dirs, never credentials. On a credentialed
workstation (the operator's always is), the case would have sailed past its asserted diagnostic
into a live multi-GB Cloud Build submission, inside the `fast` suite, on every run.
Caught by inspection before any run; fixed by short-circuiting the verb under a
`buorb_immure_resolve_only` tweak.

## The standing hazard

Nothing structural makes the fast tier credless. Every existing fast fixture is safe only
because none happened to invoke a GCP-capable colophon — the first one that did nearly fired a
live build. Future fast cases can repeat this silently: the failure mode is a *passing* fast
suite that spends money and mutates the depot.

## Triage question

Whether to enforce fast-tier credlessness by construction. Candidate shapes (not designed, just
recorded): the fast suite runner exports a poison marker that the credential-load path refuses
(loud "fast tier reached a credential load" die); or theurge's fast tier asserts no network
egress; or a per-case declaration ("reaches GCP: yes/no") validated against the tier. Each has
costs; the buo-seam-per-case convention may also be judged sufficient — but then the convention
needs stating somewhere an author of a new fast case will read it.

1	# Image-family tabtarget retirement — terminal-exclusivity debt (heat ₣BH)
2	
3	*Written 2026-06-10 while executing the image-backdoor-path-verbs pace
4	(₢BHAAa) in heat ₣BH (rbk-11-mvp-lode-universal-capture). Records a
5	deliberately-incurred terminal-exclusivity violation and the two later events
6	that clear it.*
7	
8	## Why this memo exists
9	
10	The image-backdoor-path-verbs pace introduced the generic, type-blind
11	path-polymorphic trio — **list / wrest / jettison** on raw GAR paths — and, by
12	operator decision, gave them their **final-form colophons** (the bare verb
13	letters) immediately rather than an interim name that would have to be
14	re-minted at each retirement wave:
15	
16	| Colophon | Function | Register |
17	|----------|----------|----------|
18	| `rbw-il` | `rbfl_list`     | lowercase (read) |
19	| `rbw-iw` | `rbfl_wrest`    | lowercase (read / pull) |
20	| `rbw-iJ` | `rbfl_jettison` | capital (GAR-destroying) |
21	
22	## The violation
23	
24	CLAUDE.md "Terminal Exclusivity": *a prefix either IS a name or HAS children,
25	never both.* As of this pace:
26	
27	- `rbw-iw` now **names** the generic wrest verb **and** still heads the live
28	  family `iwh` (wrest·hallmark) / `iwr` (wrest·reliquary).
29	- `rbw-iJ` now **names** the generic jettison verb **and** still heads the live
30	  family `iJh` (jettison·hallmark) / `iJr` (jettison·reliquary).
31	- `rbw-il` is clean — `list` had no prior `il*` family.
32	
33	So `iw` and `iJ` are in violation. **This is accepted on purpose** (operator,
34	2026-06-10): the alternative was a throwaway suffix (e.g. `iwx`/`iJx`) minted
35	now and renamed twice as the families empty — churn on a permanent
36	primary-universe identifier across tabtargets, the zipper, docs, and tests. The
37	violation is transient and carries the removal conditions below.
38	
39	## Removal conditions — two waves clear the debt
40	
41	The image family retires in three waves tied to namespace lifecycle. Wave 1 is
42	this pace (retire `iwe`/`iJe`, the enshrinement variants — `rbi_es` is
43	disposable and fully subsumed by the generic verbs). The remaining two waves
44	clear the terminal-exclusivity violation:
45	
46	- **Wave 2 — inscribe→conclave cutover (THIS heat, ₣BH).** When `rbi_rq`
47	  (reliquary) migrates into `rbi_ld`, retire the reliquary variants
48	  `iar` / `irr` / `iwr` / `iJr`. This removes the `iwr` / `iJr` children.
49	- **Wave 3 — made-side retrofit heat (SEPARATE heat, where Ark earns the `k`
50	  command family).** Retire the hallmark variants `iah` / `irh` / `iwh` / `iJh`.
51	  This removes `iwh` / `iJh` — the **last** children — and restores terminal
52	  exclusivity: `iw` and `iJ` become pure terminal names.
53	
54	## Semantic note — not all eight remaining variants are equal
55	
56	When the waves land, distinguish two kinds:
57	
58	- **The four wrest/jettison variants** (`iwh` `iwr` `iJh` `iJr`) are **pure
59	  pass-throughs** — they already dispatch to the very same `rbfl_wrest` /
60	  `rbfl_jettison` the generic verbs use. Zero behavior delta. They survive only
61	  to retire bundled with their namespace; functionally the generic verbs cover
62	  them the instant they exist. Deleting these is pure tabtarget + zipper removal.
63	- **The four audit/rekon variants** (`iah` `iar` `irh` `irr`) are **not** mere
64	  redundancy. `rekon` asserts canonical-member *presence* (the
65	  hallmark-lifecycle fixture asserts its non-zero exit on a missing member) and
66	  `audit_hallmarks` emits `buf_write_fact_multi` fact files. The type-blind
67	  generic `list` reproduces neither. This is why `rekon` in particular is
68	  contracted out to the made-side retrofit heat rather than collapsed into the
69	  generic layer — its semantics must be re-homed, not merely deleted.
70	
71	## Done when (this debt is cleared)
72	
73	- After Wave 3, `grep -rE 'rbw-i[wJ][hr]' tt/ Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` returns
74	  nothing; `iw` and `iJ` head no children.
75	- The Image-group comment block in `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh` no longer carries
76	  the terminal-exclusivity-violation note this pace added.
77

*Written 2026-06-10 while executing the image-backdoor-path-verbs pace
(₢BHAAa) in heat ₣BH (rbk-11-mvp-lode-universal-capture). Records a
deliberately-incurred terminal-exclusivity violation and the two later events
that clear it.*

## Why this memo exists

path-polymorphic trio — **list / wrest / jettison** on raw GAR paths — and, by
operator decision, gave them their **final-form colophons** (the bare verb
letters) immediately rather than an interim name that would have to be
re-minted at each retirement wave:

| Colophon | Function | Register |
|----------|----------|----------|
| `rbw-il` | `rbfl_list`     | lowercase (read) |
| `rbw-iw` | `rbfl_wrest`    | lowercase (read / pull) |
| `rbw-iJ` | `rbfl_jettison` | capital (GAR-destroying) |

## The violation

CLAUDE.md "Terminal Exclusivity": *a prefix either IS a name or HAS children,
never both.* As of this pace:

- `rbw-iw` now **names** the generic wrest verb **and** still heads the live
  family `iwh` (wrest·hallmark) / `iwr` (wrest·reliquary).
- `rbw-iJ` now **names** the generic jettison verb **and** still heads the live
  family `iJh` (jettison·hallmark) / `iJr` (jettison·reliquary).
- `rbw-il` is clean — `list` had no prior `il*` family.

So `iw` and `iJ` are in violation. **This is accepted on purpose** (operator,
2026-06-10): the alternative was a throwaway suffix (e.g. `iwx`/`iJx`) minted
now and renamed twice as the families empty — churn on a permanent
primary-universe identifier across tabtargets, the zipper, docs, and tests. The
violation is transient and carries the removal conditions below.

## Removal conditions — two waves clear the debt

The image family retires in three waves tied to namespace lifecycle. Wave 1 is

disposable and fully subsumed by the generic verbs). The remaining two waves

- **Wave 2 — inscribe→conclave cutover (THIS heat, ₣BH).** When `rbi_rq`

- **Wave 3 — made-side retrofit heat (SEPARATE heat, where Ark earns the `k`
  command family).** Retire the hallmark variants `iah` / `irh` / `iwh` / `iJh`.
  This removes `iwh` / `iJh` — the **last** children — and restores terminal
  exclusivity: `iw` and `iJ` become pure terminal names.

## Semantic note — not all eight remaining variants are equal

When the waves land, distinguish two kinds:

- **The four wrest/jettison variants** (`iwh` `iwr` `iJh` `iJr`) are **pure
  pass-throughs** — they already dispatch to the very same `rbfl_wrest` /
  `rbfl_jettison` the generic verbs use. Zero behavior delta. They survive only
  to retire bundled with their namespace; functionally the generic verbs cover
  them the instant they exist. Deleting these is pure tabtarget + zipper removal.
- **The four audit/rekon variants** (`iah` `iar` `irh` `irr`) are **not** mere
  redundancy. `rekon` asserts canonical-member *presence* (the
  hallmark-lifecycle fixture asserts its non-zero exit on a missing member) and
  `audit_hallmarks` emits `buf_write_fact_multi` fact files. The type-blind
  generic `list` reproduces neither. This is why `rekon` in particular is
  contracted out to the made-side retrofit heat rather than collapsed into the
  generic layer — its semantics must be re-homed, not merely deleted.

## Done when (this debt is cleared)

1	# Choosing Quoins Well: An LLM Introspective Report on Prefix-Identifier Minting
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Discovery draft v0.1 — Introspective report (2026-06-10)
6	
7	## Origin
8	
9	This memo records introspections offered by Claude (Fable 5) during a review of MCM,
10	AXLA, and their consuming specifications (BUS0, RBS0, JJS0), in conversation with the
11	editor. The editor's stated goal: choose minting conventions that let a model hold
12	more of the system in context with fewer symbols. This memo is the model's attempt to
13	report, as honestly as it can, what prefix-discriminated identifiers (quoins) actually
14	do to its processing — and what minting choices follow.
15	
16	Sibling to `memo-20260110-acronym-selection-study.md` (the empirical minting study);
17	where that memo reasons from observed collisions, this one reasons from the inside.
18	
19	## Epistemic Stance
20	
21	Everything below is hypothesis, not measurement. An LLM's reports about its own
22	processing are plausible reconstructions consistent with its architecture and its
23	observed behavior — they are not privileged access. Where a claim is backed by
24	behavior observable in the originating conversation, the specimen is named. Where it
25	is architectural reasoning, it is marked as such. The Diptych memo's phrase "direct
26	introspection" deserves the same caveat retroactively.
27	
28	Confidence ordering of the four core claims, highest first: interference (§4),
29	certain coreference (§2), front-loaded learning (§3), cross-kit transfer (§5).
30	
31	## 1. The Frame: Ambiguity Count, Not Token Count
32	
33	The intuitive model of context economy — fewer tokens, more room — is not where
34	quoins pay. The expensive thing about holding a large specification in context is the
35	number of *unresolved bindings* the reader must keep alive. Plain prose says "the
36	record," "that field," "the heat's entry," and each such phrase forces maintenance of
37	a candidate-referent cloud that collapses only when later context disambiguates — or
38	never collapses, and silently mis-binds.
39	
40	A quoin collapses the cloud at the point of reading. `jjdhm_silks` is exactly one
41	thing; every occurrence is certainly the same thing; attention can bind all
42	occurrences together without spending anything on coreference hypotheses. The word
43	`silks` alone would collide (Heat member and Tack member both carry one); the prefix
44	settles it immediately.
45	
46	**Claim: a quoin converts probabilistic coreference into certain coreference, and
47	certainty — not brevity — is what frees capacity.** This is the deep win of the
48	mapping-as-spine design, and it survives any change of surface syntax (AsciiDoc,
49	Diptych recto/verso, guillemet lectio).
50	
51	## 2. How Prefixes Are Actually Read
52	
53	### Tokenization honesty
54	
55	A prefix like `jjdhm_` is not one token. It is two or three odd subword chunks, and
56	which chunks is tokenizer-dependent and unintuitive. The Diptych memo's token
57	arithmetic is directionally right about ceremony but should not be extended to assume
58	prefixes are atomic.
59	
60	What matters more than the count: **the same string always tokenizes the same way**.
61	A prefix family is a stable composite shape. The first few encounters are spelled out
62	against the legend; after that the shape is recognized, not decoded. The cost is
63	front-loaded and amortizes across occurrences.
64	
65	### Two consequences for minting
66	
67	- **Legend reachability.** A prefix is only free when its legend is in context.
68	  Partial reads of large documents — and Diptych's planned section-level
69	  presentation — can strand body text away from its category declarations. Whatever
70	  the format becomes, the legend must travel with the slice. (Lectio compilation
71	  should treat the relevant legend rows as mandatory cargo.)
72	- **Zipf-shaped brevity.** Quoins used constantly deserve the shortest, most
73	  distinctive names, because their learning cost amortizes across hundreds of
74	  bindings. Rare quoins can afford long descriptive names; they will be spelled out
75	  on every encounter anyway, so their length should be spent on self-description.
76	
77	## 3. Interference: Distinctiveness Beats Systematicity
78	
79	This is the highest-confidence claim, and the one with teeth.
80	
81	The mechanisms that make an LLM good at repeated-pattern recall key on string
82	*shape*. Reading proceeds as sequence early and as shape once familiar — and the
83	transition happens around one chunk (roughly 3–5 characters). Two long identifiers
84	sharing a long common head with a single discriminating letter in the interior are
85	nearly the same retrieval key. That is not merely "hard to read" — it is a setup for
86	genuine mis-binding, where attention retrieves the wrong family's context and the
87	model confidently continues down the wrong branch.
88	
89	Specimens from the originating review:
90	
91	- `axhempt_typed_parameter` vs `axhopt_typed_parameter` (AXLA hierarchy markers):
92	  shared head `axh`, shared tail `pt_typed_parameter`, discriminator one interior
93	  letter. The model flagged these as requiring deliberate spelling-out on every
94	  encounter.
95	- JJS0's historical `rd` collision (three quoins, three meanings — recorded in the
96	  acronym-selection study as Pattern J's founding failure): the same failure mode at
97	  length two.
98	
99	The taxonomic letter-chain (`h`+`e`+`m`+`p`+`t` = hierarchy→entity→method→
100	parameter→typed) optimizes for the legend-writer: every edge of the tree is encoded.
101	But the reader's machinery wants **maximal early divergence between things that mean
102	differently**. If two families differ in meaning, they should differ in their first
103	chunk, not their fourth letter.
104	
105	**Corollary — don't re-encode positional context.** Hierarchy markers appear nested
106	under their parent marker; the entity-vs-operation branch is already carried by
107	document position. Re-encoding ancestry in the child's name recreates information
108	that position already provides (the naming analogue of ACG's allocation discipline:
109	reference the home, don't recreate). Deep taxa should drop inherited letters and
110	keep only the local discriminator.
111	
112	**Corollary — the ceiling is chunkability, not count.** A 3-letter ceiling and a
113	5-letter ceiling are both proxies. The honest constraints are: (a) a prefix should
114	parse as at most ~2 chunks (in practice ≤4–5 characters after the lexicon root), and
115	(b) same-length prefixes within one document should differ by more than a single
116	interior letter. Both are mechanically checkable — see §7.
117	
118	## 4. Transfer: Cross-Kit Rhyme Is the Cheapest Multiplier
119	
120	Within each kit the sub-letters are monosemous (JJS0's discipline), but the schemes
121	only partially rhyme *across* kits: JJS0 speaks `r` record / `m` member / `e` enum;
122	RBS0's older strata speak `rbtga_`/`rbtgo_`; BUS0 has its own weave.
123	
124	Observed behavior: where kit letters rhyme with AXLA's category letters (`r` ~
125	`axr_`, `t` ~ `axt_`, `o` ~ `axo_`), a never-before-seen kit's mapping section reads
126	nearly cold — JJS0's record/member/enum families were instantly legible because the
127	convention transferred from AXLA.
128	
129	**Recommendation: promote a small repo-global sub-letter alphabet — the AXLA
130	category letters as the canonical rhyme — so that every new kit's vocabulary arrives
131	pre-learned.** Within-kit monosemy protects one document; repo-global monosemy makes
132	the whole system's vocabulary one learning event instead of N.
133	
134	## 5. Word-Part Craft
135	
136	- **Real-word stems after the underscore.** `_silks`, `_ensconce`, `_paddock`
137	  tokenize as real words and carry meaning intrinsically. Abbreviated stems (`_slk`)
138	  save characters and cost recognition — the wrong trade everywhere except the very
139	  highest-frequency quoins.
140	- **The liturgical/equestrian vocabulary is genuinely good for the model** — this
141	  deserves saying because it could look like whimsy. Rare-but-real words (ensconce,
142	  reliquary, coronet, lectio) are simultaneously distinctive (low collision with
143	  ambient technical prose, strong retrieval keys) and meaningful (the metaphor does
144	  real semantic work). Common technical words (`manager`, `handler`, `info`) are the
145	  opposite on both axes.
146	- **Attribute-name/display-text divergence is a carried cost.** Each divergence
147	  (`jjdcm_basis` → "commit") is one extra translation pair held in context.
148	  Sometimes it is load-bearing — the quoin/sprue distinction between conceptual name
149	  and wire name exists precisely for this. Where it is not load-bearing, convergence
150	  is free capacity.
151	
152	## 6. Distilled Minting Guidance
153	
154	Mint for:
155	
156	1. **Certain binding** — global uniqueness, one string per concept, terminal
157	   exclusivity (existing discipline; it is the foundation and it is right).
158	2. **Early divergence** — different meanings diverge in the first chunk; interior
159	   single-letter discrimination between long shared heads is forbidden.
160	3. **Transferable convention** — one repo-global sub-letter alphabet, anchored to
161	   AXLA's category letters; within-domain monosemy becomes cross-domain monosemy.
162	4. **Zipf-shaped brevity** — short and sharp for the frequent, long and
163	   self-describing for the rare.
164	5. **Real words in the word-part** — distinctive rare words over abbreviations;
165	   spend depth in the word, not in the prefix letters.
166	6. **Position over re-encoding** — nested/hierarchical markers carry only their
167	   local discriminator; ancestry lives in document structure.
168	
169	## 7. Mechanical Rules for a Future Validator
170	
171	Each guidance point above has a checkable form; recorded here so the eventual VOS
172	flowering can pick them up:
173	
174	- **Legend coverage**: every category prefix used in a mapping section appears in
175	  the category-declaration legend (the JJS0 `jjsz_`/`jjezs_` drift class).
176	- **Anchor↔attribute bijection**: every attribute reference resolves to an anchor
177	  and vice versa (the MCM `mcm_mapping_scrub` dangling-reference class).
178	- **Interior edit distance**: no two same-length prefixes in one document differ by
179	  exactly one interior character (the `axhempt_`/`axhopt_` and `rd` classes).
180	- **Chunk ceiling**: prefix body after the project root ≤ 4–5 characters; longer
181	  chains must justify themselves or move discrimination into the word-part.
182	- **Legend cargo** (Diptych-era): any compiled partial view (lectio, section slice)
183	  includes the legend rows for every prefix family present in the slice.
184	
185	## 8. Relation to Diptych and Reminting
186	
187	Nothing here argues for renaming anything now. The Diptych direction — identifier-is-
188	the-reference, no substitution engine — makes a future remint a mechanical rewrite,
189	and the editor's intended repo-global reminting tooling makes accumulated misminting
190	(e.g., the `jjezs_` oddity, the AXLA hierarchy chains) cheap to sweep later. The
191	right moment to apply this memo is at that tooling's design, and at each fresh mint
192	between now and then.
193	
194	The one principle that should not wait: **new mints can follow §6 immediately**, at
195	zero migration cost — the guidance constrains births, not the living.
196	
197	---
198	
199	*Written by Claude Fable 5 at the editor's request, first session of that model in
200	this repository. The memo attempts to honor the house rule that introspective
201	reports be offered as observable weather, not certified fact: every claim above is
202	falsifiable in principle by behavioral testing, and the confidence ordering in the
203	Epistemic Stance section is part of the report.*

# Choosing Quoins Well: An LLM Introspective Report on Prefix-Identifier Minting

## Status

Discovery draft v0.1 — Introspective report (2026-06-10)

## Origin

This memo records introspections offered by Claude (Fable 5) during a review of MCM,
AXLA, and their consuming specifications (BUS0, RBS0, JJS0), in conversation with the
editor. The editor's stated goal: choose minting conventions that let a model hold
more of the system in context with fewer symbols. This memo is the model's attempt to
report, as honestly as it can, what prefix-discriminated identifiers (quoins) actually
do to its processing — and what minting choices follow.

Sibling to `memo-20260110-acronym-selection-study.md` (the empirical minting study);
where that memo reasons from observed collisions, this one reasons from the inside.

## Epistemic Stance

Everything below is hypothesis, not measurement. An LLM's reports about its own
processing are plausible reconstructions consistent with its architecture and its
observed behavior — they are not privileged access. Where a claim is backed by
behavior observable in the originating conversation, the specimen is named. Where it
is architectural reasoning, it is marked as such. The Diptych memo's phrase "direct
introspection" deserves the same caveat retroactively.

Confidence ordering of the four core claims, highest first: interference (§4),
certain coreference (§2), front-loaded learning (§3), cross-kit transfer (§5).

## 1. The Frame: Ambiguity Count, Not Token Count

The intuitive model of context economy — fewer tokens, more room — is not where
quoins pay. The expensive thing about holding a large specification in context is the
number of *unresolved bindings* the reader must keep alive. Plain prose says "the
record," "that field," "the heat's entry," and each such phrase forces maintenance of
a candidate-referent cloud that collapses only when later context disambiguates — or
never collapses, and silently mis-binds.

A quoin collapses the cloud at the point of reading. `jjdhm_silks` is exactly one
thing; every occurrence is certainly the same thing; attention can bind all
occurrences together without spending anything on coreference hypotheses. The word
`silks` alone would collide (Heat member and Tack member both carry one); the prefix
settles it immediately.

**Claim: a quoin converts probabilistic coreference into certain coreference, and
certainty — not brevity — is what frees capacity.** This is the deep win of the
mapping-as-spine design, and it survives any change of surface syntax (AsciiDoc,
Diptych recto/verso, guillemet lectio).

## 2. How Prefixes Are Actually Read

### Tokenization honesty

A prefix like `jjdhm_` is not one token. It is two or three odd subword chunks, and
which chunks is tokenizer-dependent and unintuitive. The Diptych memo's token
arithmetic is directionally right about ceremony but should not be extended to assume
prefixes are atomic.

What matters more than the count: **the same string always tokenizes the same way**.
A prefix family is a stable composite shape. The first few encounters are spelled out
against the legend; after that the shape is recognized, not decoded. The cost is
front-loaded and amortizes across occurrences.

### Two consequences for minting

- **Legend reachability.** A prefix is only free when its legend is in context.
  Partial reads of large documents — and Diptych's planned section-level
  presentation — can strand body text away from its category declarations. Whatever
  the format becomes, the legend must travel with the slice. (Lectio compilation
  should treat the relevant legend rows as mandatory cargo.)
- **Zipf-shaped brevity.** Quoins used constantly deserve the shortest, most
  distinctive names, because their learning cost amortizes across hundreds of
  bindings. Rare quoins can afford long descriptive names; they will be spelled out
  on every encounter anyway, so their length should be spent on self-description.

## 3. Interference: Distinctiveness Beats Systematicity

This is the highest-confidence claim, and the one with teeth.

The mechanisms that make an LLM good at repeated-pattern recall key on string
*shape*. Reading proceeds as sequence early and as shape once familiar — and the
transition happens around one chunk (roughly 3–5 characters). Two long identifiers
sharing a long common head with a single discriminating letter in the interior are
nearly the same retrieval key. That is not merely "hard to read" — it is a setup for
genuine mis-binding, where attention retrieves the wrong family's context and the
model confidently continues down the wrong branch.

Specimens from the originating review:

- `axhempt_typed_parameter` vs `axhopt_typed_parameter` (AXLA hierarchy markers):
  shared head `axh`, shared tail `pt_typed_parameter`, discriminator one interior
  letter. The model flagged these as requiring deliberate spelling-out on every
  encounter.
- JJS0's historical `rd` collision (three quoins, three meanings — recorded in the
  acronym-selection study as Pattern J's founding failure): the same failure mode at
  length two.

The taxonomic letter-chain (`h`+`e`+`m`+`p`+`t` = hierarchy→entity→method→
parameter→typed) optimizes for the legend-writer: every edge of the tree is encoded.
But the reader's machinery wants **maximal early divergence between things that mean
differently**. If two families differ in meaning, they should differ in their first
chunk, not their fourth letter.

**Corollary — don't re-encode positional context.** Hierarchy markers appear nested
under their parent marker; the entity-vs-operation branch is already carried by
document position. Re-encoding ancestry in the child's name recreates information
that position already provides (the naming analogue of ACG's allocation discipline:
reference the home, don't recreate). Deep taxa should drop inherited letters and
keep only the local discriminator.

**Corollary — the ceiling is chunkability, not count.** A 3-letter ceiling and a
5-letter ceiling are both proxies. The honest constraints are: (a) a prefix should
parse as at most ~2 chunks (in practice ≤4–5 characters after the lexicon root), and
(b) same-length prefixes within one document should differ by more than a single
interior letter. Both are mechanically checkable — see §7.

## 4. Transfer: Cross-Kit Rhyme Is the Cheapest Multiplier

Within each kit the sub-letters are monosemous (JJS0's discipline), but the schemes
only partially rhyme *across* kits: JJS0 speaks `r` record / `m` member / `e` enum;
RBS0's older strata speak `rbtga_`/`rbtgo_`; BUS0 has its own weave.

Observed behavior: where kit letters rhyme with AXLA's category letters (`r` ~
`axr_`, `t` ~ `axt_`, `o` ~ `axo_`), a never-before-seen kit's mapping section reads
nearly cold — JJS0's record/member/enum families were instantly legible because the
convention transferred from AXLA.

**Recommendation: promote a small repo-global sub-letter alphabet — the AXLA
category letters as the canonical rhyme — so that every new kit's vocabulary arrives
pre-learned.** Within-kit monosemy protects one document; repo-global monosemy makes
the whole system's vocabulary one learning event instead of N.

## 5. Word-Part Craft

- **Real-word stems after the underscore.** `_silks`, `_ensconce`, `_paddock`
  tokenize as real words and carry meaning intrinsically. Abbreviated stems (`_slk`)
  save characters and cost recognition — the wrong trade everywhere except the very
  highest-frequency quoins.
- **The liturgical/equestrian vocabulary is genuinely good for the model** — this
  deserves saying because it could look like whimsy. Rare-but-real words (ensconce,
  reliquary, coronet, lectio) are simultaneously distinctive (low collision with
  ambient technical prose, strong retrieval keys) and meaningful (the metaphor does
  real semantic work). Common technical words (`manager`, `handler`, `info`) are the
  opposite on both axes.
- **Attribute-name/display-text divergence is a carried cost.** Each divergence
  (`jjdcm_basis` → "commit") is one extra translation pair held in context.
  Sometimes it is load-bearing — the quoin/sprue distinction between conceptual name
  and wire name exists precisely for this. Where it is not load-bearing, convergence
  is free capacity.

## 6. Distilled Minting Guidance

Mint for:

1. **Certain binding** — global uniqueness, one string per concept, terminal
   exclusivity (existing discipline; it is the foundation and it is right).
2. **Early divergence** — different meanings diverge in the first chunk; interior
   single-letter discrimination between long shared heads is forbidden.
3. **Transferable convention** — one repo-global sub-letter alphabet, anchored to
   AXLA's category letters; within-domain monosemy becomes cross-domain monosemy.
4. **Zipf-shaped brevity** — short and sharp for the frequent, long and
   self-describing for the rare.
5. **Real words in the word-part** — distinctive rare words over abbreviations;
   spend depth in the word, not in the prefix letters.
6. **Position over re-encoding** — nested/hierarchical markers carry only their
   local discriminator; ancestry lives in document structure.

## 7. Mechanical Rules for a Future Validator

Each guidance point above has a checkable form; recorded here so the eventual VOS
flowering can pick them up:

- **Legend coverage**: every category prefix used in a mapping section appears in
  the category-declaration legend (the JJS0 `jjsz_`/`jjezs_` drift class).
- **Anchor↔attribute bijection**: every attribute reference resolves to an anchor
  and vice versa (the MCM `mcm_mapping_scrub` dangling-reference class).
- **Interior edit distance**: no two same-length prefixes in one document differ by
  exactly one interior character (the `axhempt_`/`axhopt_` and `rd` classes).
- **Chunk ceiling**: prefix body after the project root ≤ 4–5 characters; longer
  chains must justify themselves or move discrimination into the word-part.
- **Legend cargo** (Diptych-era): any compiled partial view (lectio, section slice)
  includes the legend rows for every prefix family present in the slice.

## 8. Relation to Diptych and Reminting

Nothing here argues for renaming anything now. The Diptych direction — identifier-is-
the-reference, no substitution engine — makes a future remint a mechanical rewrite,
and the editor's intended repo-global reminting tooling makes accumulated misminting
(e.g., the `jjezs_` oddity, the AXLA hierarchy chains) cheap to sweep later. The
right moment to apply this memo is at that tooling's design, and at each fresh mint
between now and then.

The one principle that should not wait: **new mints can follow §6 immediately**, at
zero migration cost — the guidance constrains births, not the living.

---

*Written by Claude Fable 5 at the editor's request, first session of that model in
this repository. The memo attempts to honor the house rule that introspective
reports be offered as observable weather, not certified fact: every claim above is
falsifiable in principle by behavioral testing, and the confidence ordering in the
Epistemic Stance section is part of the report.*

pub static RBTDRC_SUITES: &[rbtdre_Suite] = &[
    // Fast — no external dependencies.
    rbtdre_Suite {
        name: "fast",
        fixtures: &[
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION,
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_REGIME_VALIDATION,
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_REGIME_SMOKE,
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_PODVM_RESOLVE,
            &crate::rbtdrf_handbook::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_HANDBOOK_RENDER,
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_DOCKERFILE_HYGIENE,
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_FOUNDRY_PATH,
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_RECIPE_VALIDATION,
            &crate::rbtdru_cupel::RBTDRU_FIXTURE_CUPEL,
            &crate::rbtdrn_conformance::RBTDRN_FIXTURE_CONFORMANCE,
        ],
    },
    // Service — fast + GCP-credentialed bare fixtures.
    rbtdre_Suite {
        name: "service",
        fixtures: &[
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_ENROLLMENT_VALIDATION,
            &crate::rbtdrf_fast::RBTDRF_FIXTURE_REGIME_VALIDATION,

content.text:
All 15 processed, claims spot-verified in code (the skirmish/gauntlet subset drift is real — both miss `handbook-render`/`foundry-path`/`recipe-validation`; the delete builder is still floating `:latest`; Wave 2 of the image-family retirement is confirmed already landed). Here is the full triage. **Nine slated paces, two RBSHR entries, four declines-with-record.**

## Slate — tonight, before the ladder (worker-drivable, all small)

1. **suite-subset-repair** (durations memo, finding 1+2) — add the three missing fast fixtures to gauntlet+skirmish in `RBTDRC_SUITES`; refresh CLAUDE.md's stale test table. Tiny, compile-checked. **Must land first — tonight's skirmish should run the corrected subset.**
2. **cloud-delete-hardening** (bundles 4 memos: pin-delete-builder, jettison-404, URLError, urllib-timeout) — all four touch the same delete surface, all verified live by the ladder's banish/abjure traffic. Pin the builder by digest (the Director-identity asymmetry break is the one real security item in the corpus); make jettison's code match its idempotent message; catch URLError in `fire_delete`; bound every `urlopen`. One pace, one review.
3. **immure-residue** (immure-noncritical items 1, 2, 6, 7, 8) — rbgjl07 non-empty asserts, `< /dev/null` stdin belt in rbgjl08/09, the quay-CDN line in rbw-di's egress inventory, the 4KB-vs-8-leaf envelope arithmetic check, and the RBSL family block in claude-rbk-acronyms.md. Mechanical bundle.
4. **elect-anchor-slot-count-soften** — mirror the landed brand fix: log-and-leave instead of `buc_die` on slot-count≠1. Tiny, surgical, same pattern that just shipped. The dormancy is real but the fix is cheaper than re-finding the bug live next year.

## Slate — tomorrow (fine after the ladder; fast-tier or test-infra verification)

5. **invest-actas-readback** — extend the rbk-08 read-back pattern to the self-actAs grant. Doesn't need the live flap signature; harmless on a settled depot. Live proof rides your depot-recreate plan.
6. **fast-tier-credless-poison** — the near-miss was a fast case one inspection away from firing live builds on every `fast` run. Convention isn't enough; the cheap shape (fast runner exports a poison marker the credential-load path refuses) is small and self-verifying. Worth a pace.
7. **cupel-python-walk** — import allowlist + subprocess argv[0] scan against `ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED`; adjudicate `gcloud` minimally (allowlist with dated comment, don't convert rbgjv02 now); include the ten-minute expander probe. Test-infra only — no cloud surface, no ladder dependency.
8. **enshrine-spec-residue** (structural-residue items 2+3) — retire RBSIA's Enshrinements-Audit section + `gar_enshrines_namespace` quoin (depot is disposable, so legacy rbi_es auditing has no future subject); collapse `rbst_reliquary_stamp` into `rbst_touchmark`. **Must land before ₢BHAAD (the scrub).** Item 1 is confirmed scrub-owned, no action.
9. **single-slot-extract-consolidation** — my judgment on the open question: the per-kind premise protects *vocabulary*, not extract *implementation*; three byte-parallel copies of the same extract is exactly the surface where the drift class fired today. Consolidate (memo's option 2: one parameterized single-slot extract; bole stays separate). Tomorrow with AAR, sharing one post-landing service run — the verification-batching posture.

## RBSHR entries (record, don't spend)

- **convergence-deadline-shape** — real but latent: today's worst web is 55 versions against a ~1000 threshold, and the depot is disposable. Horizon entry, revisit if web sizes grow.
- **image-family Wave 3** — belongs to the made-side retrofit heat by the memo's own structure; RBSHR carries the obligation so the violation note in the zipper isn't the only record.

## Decline with record

- **immure items 3/4/5** (dead-key forfeit, CBp floor accretion, RBRA census) — already serve as records; nothing to do.
- **durations extraction** (read-only-op spans) — low value; the memo itself is the manifest and the hist logs persist. The ladder wrap records the new suite baseline per its docket.
- **quoin-minting-introspection** — not a finding; it's a standing reference doc for future minting/tooling. Stands as-is.

**One structural call for you:** this plan runs the ladder tonight with paces 5–9 plus ₢BHAAR/₢BHAAb/₢BHAAD landing after it. Defensible — everything post-ladder is host-side, spec, or test-infra, verified at fast tier, with AAR + extract-consolidation sharing one service run tomorrow; skirmish's named gate (the bind-mode build) is already landed by AAX. I'll record the inversion by reslating a note into the ladder and AAR dockets. The alternative is holding the ladder for tomorrow night — cleaner ordering, but you lose tonight's overnight window.

Say go (or adjust any verdict) and I'll slate all nine and write the two RBSHR entries now.

# jjezs_slate cloud-delete-hardening
## Character
Four small repairs on one delete surface, bundled for one review and one live gate
(the endgame ladder's banish/abjure traffic covers them).
Tier: sonnet-delegable.

## Goal
- Pin ZRBFC_DELETE_BUILDER by digest (rbfc0_FoundryCore.sh) — it is the only floating
  builder running under a delete-privileged identity (Director SA);
  record the pin in RBSCB's cloud-dispatch posture section.
- rbfl_jettison (rbfld_Delete.sh): tolerate 404 as success — make the code match the
  "Jettisoned or nonexistent" message; idempotent delete is the house shape.
- rbgjl06-package-delete.py fire_delete: catch URLError alongside HTTPError
  (HTTPError subclasses URLError — structure the handler accordingly),
  log in the reconciling form, continue; the absence poll arbitrates.
- rbgjl06: explicit timeout (~30s) on every urlopen; fire_delete tolerates the timeout,
  truth-readers (package_absent, list_version_ids) and metadata_token die loud.

## Done when
All four landed; build + shellcheck + fast green.
Live coverage is structurally delegated to the endgame-verification ladder pace.

## Sources
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-pin-delete-builder.md
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-jettison-404-honesty.md
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-urlerror-tolerance.md
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-urllib-timeout.md

# jjezs_slate elect-anchor-slot-count-soften
## Character
Tiny surgical repair mirroring the landed kind-brand fix (commit 91666a97b).
Tier: sonnet-delegable.

## Goal
zrbfd_elect_base_anchor (rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh): when the populated
RBRV_IMAGE_n_ORIGIN slot count is not exactly one, log-and-leave-the-ANCHOR
(loud buc_log_args), not buc_die — absence of election is already a normal outcome
of this function, and the die kills a multi-origin ordain behind a chained bole fact
(dormant today: no live vessel populates slots 2-3; fires on the first that does).
Decide at mount whether count==0 warrants a distinct message from count>1.

## Done when
The die is softened to log-and-leave; fast green;
a fixture case covers the multi-slot no-op if one is cheap to add.

## Sources

# jjezs_slate fast-tier-credless-by-construction
## Character
Hazard-closure design + implement; small but cross-cutting (theurge suite runner +
credential load path). Tier: sonnet-delegable with care —
the failure mode it closes is a PASSING fast suite that spends money and mutates the depot.

## Goal
Make fast-tier credlessness structural rather than conventional:
the fast suite runner exports a poison marker and the credential-load path refuses under it
(loud die naming the violation), so a fast case that reaches a credential load fails the
suite instead of firing a live build.
Exact shape is a mount-time choice (poison env var is the cheap candidate from the memo);
whatever lands, state the convention where an author of a new fast case will read it.

## Done when
A fast case reaching credential load dies loud by construction; fast suite green;
the convention documented in the theurge/test authoring context.

## Sources
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fast-tier-credless-by-convention.md
(the near-miss record: the podvm resolve case was one inspection away from live multi-GB
submissions on every fast run; the buorb_immure_resolve_only seam was the spot fix).

# jjezs_slate single-slot-extract-consolidation
## Character
Behavior-preserving consolidation; the judgment was settled at triage:
the per-kind-verbs premise protects vocabulary, not extract implementation,
and the propagation-drift class fired on exactly this block
(the rbls_ sprue sweep missed one of the three copies and broke the service suite same-day).
Tier: sonnet-delegable, opus-grade review.

## Goal
One shared single-slot extract (prefix / brand / label parameterized) consumed by
underpin, conclave, and immure — replacing the three byte-parallel copies in
rbldw_Underpin.sh, rbldr_Reliquary.sh, rbldv_Immure.sh, including the keys-dump diagnostic.
rbldb_Bole.sh stays separate: its multi-slot 1..3 continue-on-empty loop is genuinely
different shape.
The helper's home is a mount-time choice — kind data arrives as parameters,
so a spine-adjacent home does not violate spine-owns-no-kind-knowledge.

## Done when
Three copies replaced by one; build + shellcheck + fast green.
Live coverage shares the next service-tier run (verification batching),
never a dedicated run of its own.

## Sources
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-extract-keys-triplication.md (option 2 chosen at triage).
