content.content:
1	[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
2	==== Horizon Roadmap
3	
4	Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
5	Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).
6	
7	Items here are not planned for any heat.
8	When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
9	This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.
10	
11	**SA numeric ID for member references.**
12	Google recommends using a
13	{giam_service_account}'s
14	numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
15	Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
16	(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
17	backoff profile) is necessary.
18	Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
19	Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
20	_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
21	_Reference_:
22	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
23	(SA propagation section, evidence table).
24	
25	**Read-back verification universalization.**
26	Currently only
27	{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
28	performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
29	{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
30	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
31	and
32	{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
33	trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
34	Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
35	_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
36	_Reference_:
37	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
38	(Convergence Analysis section).
39	
40	**SA preflight universalization.**
41	Currently only
42	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
43	performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
44	{giam_service_account}.
45	Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
46	_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
47	_Reference_:
48	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
49	(Convergence Analysis section).
50	
51	**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
52	Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
53	Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
54	Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
55	_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
56	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
57	RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).
58	
59	**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
60	Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
61	`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
62	Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
63	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
64	_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
65	containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).
66	
67	**Substrate Lode consumption — wsl/podvm host-side election and provisioning.**
68	Lode capture is landed: per-kind capture verbs (`ensconce`/`conclave`/`underpin`/`immure` — `enshrine` retired) each emit one provenance-enveloped GAR package, the **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_ark} on the built side and identified by its touchmark;
69	intent stays distributed per kind — no unified recipe noun, no resolved-FQIN field — generalizing RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern.
70	What remains is the consumption half for the substrate kinds: the workload's `wsl --import` consuming a verified rootfs Lode in place of the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on, and podman machine provisioning consuming a podvm Lode in place of quay's rapidly-churning upstream.
71	Substrate touchmarks are Director-pinned at the nameplate and materialized at charge — the Retriever receives versions, never elects them.
72	Open: which host-tier regime backs node-side election/provisioning (station regime vs BURN node profile vs a new host-config regime).
73	_Trigger_: when the Windows host workload moves onto the project-controlled WSL distro, or when podman machine provisioning must survive quay retention.
74	_Reference_: RBSLU-lode_underpin.adoc, RBSLI-lode_immure.adoc (the substrate capture specs);
75	RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc (build-time election precedent);
76	BUSJCW CaparisonWindows, BUSJGW GarrisonWsl (the `wsl --export` seed path this retires).
77	
78	**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
79	Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
80	Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
81	The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
82	The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
83	Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
84	No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
85	Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
86	Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
87	_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
88	_Reference_: prior design conversation capture (paddock).
89	
90	**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
91	A
92	{rbtgi_depot}
93	holding alongside the
94	{rbtga_ark}
95	that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
96	GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
97	Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
98	Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
99	auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
100	+
101	Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
102	{at_bottle_container}
103	at charge time, rather than baking models into
104	{at_bottle_image_s}.
105	Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
106	Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
107	{at_nameplate}-declared
108	hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
109	_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
110	{at_bottle_container}
111	against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
112	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
113	design conversation pending heat.
114	
115	**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
116	The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
117	This is intentional: the spec defines
118	{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
119	as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
120	The DNS allowlist
121	({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
122	provides name-level precision;
123	the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
124	These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
125	+
126	The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
127	IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
128	The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
129	A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
130	+
131	Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
132	+
133	. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
134	  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
135	  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
136	  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
137	  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
138	. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
139	  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
140	  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
141	  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
142	. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
143	  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
144	  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
145	  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
146	+
147	_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
148	_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
149	the tadmor nameplate's {rbrn_regime} assignment file (current CIDR configuration).
150	
151	**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
152	Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
153	The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
154	WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
155	New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
156	VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
157	_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
158	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).
159	NOTE: WireGuard-in-sentry is **rejected** by the current shaping — `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` §11 moves the tunnel's local end out of the sentry (whose containment-harness purpose it would invert) into a dedicated terminus vessel, the local end of the *Outpost* (`raise`/`strike`); the reach object's current vocabulary and staging live in that memo.
160	
161	**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
162	A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
163	Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
164	Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
165	AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
166	the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
167	_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
168	Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
169	tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
170	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode); current shaping `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` (the *Quire* — allowlist MVP, Outpost fast-follow, consume-side governance).
171	
172	**Bottle credential custody.**
173	Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
174	The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.
175	This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
176	The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.
177	Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
178	_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
179	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table); the Bedrock instance of this pattern is the Quire's confined-credential identity layer (`Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` §6).
180	
181	NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
182	The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
183	The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
184	Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.
185	
186	**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
187	Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
188	AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
189	If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
190	Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
191	_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
192	_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.
193	
194	**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
195	An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
196	Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
197	{rbtgi_depot}
198	creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
199	Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
200	the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
201	_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
202	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
203	design conversation pending heat.
204	
205	**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
206	A
207	{giam_service_account}
208	in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
209	The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
210	it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
211	Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
212	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
213	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
214	
215	**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
216	Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
217	A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
218	Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
219	bullions are the new AWS-side holding with S3-native retention semantics.
220	Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
221	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
222	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
223	Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.
224	
225	**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
226	Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
227	Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
228	Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
229	A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
230	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
231	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
232	
233	**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
234	Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
235	The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
236	Director holds no AWS credentials;
237	failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
238	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
239	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
240	
241	**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
242	Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
243	A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
244	{at_bottle_container}
245	over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
246	Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
247	Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
248	_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
249	near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
250	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
251	`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
252	Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
253	NOTE: the native-consume half of this entry is renamed and currently shaped as the **Quire** in `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` (vocabulary, privacy boundaries, consume-side governance: Precentor/Chorister/Prebend/Cloister, deployment ladder); the custom-model-import half stays with the embassy/bullion delivery path above and remains unshaped.
254	
255	**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED.**
256	Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
257	Operation-level routing: Lode capture uses tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
258	Lodes (builder tools and base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
259	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).
260	
261	**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
262	Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
263	Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
264	_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
265	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
266	Cross-reference correction (`Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md`): the conduit/Outpost VPC is an *AWS* network and VPC-SC is a *GCP* service perimeter around the depot project — different clouds; neither can serve the other. The two items are independent.
267	
268	**Cosign image signing.**
269	Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
270	KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
271	Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
272	_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
273	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).
274	
275	**Lode vouch signing — confirm GAR referrers, decide cosign-vs-referrers.**
276	The Lode provenance envelope rides today as a reserved `:rbi_vouch` tag (one per Lode), schema-versioned with `signature: null` — a clean upgrade seam reserved without spending now.
277	When signing becomes warranted, confirm whether GAR's OCI 1.1 referrers API is mature enough to attach attestation as a subject-linked artifact, and decide between that and cosign for the Lode vouch.
278	Verifying GAR referrers maturity is a vendor (Palisade) question with no consumer until distribution leaves the project, so it is deliberately not a pace.
279	_Trigger_: same as Cosign — external image distribution or builder strategy beyond Google Cloud Build.
280	_Reference_: RBS0-SpecTop.adoc (Lode provenance envelope, `rbld-vouch-2`).
281	
282	**Binary Authorization.**
283	Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
284	Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
285	Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
286	_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed container platforms.
287	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).
288	
289	**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
290	Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in vessel build contexts are not validated.
291	A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04` uses a mutable tag.
292	Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
293	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
294	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).
295	
296	**Reproducibility verification.**
297	Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.
298	Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
299	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
300	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).
301	
302	**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
303	Recipe Bottle grants
304	{rbtgi_depot}
305	capabilities to identities the depot's IAM recognizes.
306	The shipping model is the keyfile tier: each operator role holds a service-account key file (the
307	{rbtgi_rbra_file})
308	on disk, possession of the key is the identity, and no GCP organization is required.
309	Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
310	The depot owner configures the
311	{rbtgi_depot}
312	to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
313	The
314	{rbtgi_depot}
315	remains the home of the participant roster (the role-to-identity mapping); the IdP owns only identity proofing.
316	Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
317	{rbtr_payor}
318	cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
319	A qualifying organization is free via Cloud Identity once its owner verifies control of a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.
320	The Google-side credential path is curl-native — an STS token exchange (RFC 8693), no gcloud and no added stack dependency; only the IdP-auth leg (device flow where the issuer supports it) varies by issuer.
321	Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
322	{at_operator}
323	*access* identity arriving the other way.
324	_Trigger_: first concrete need from an organization that cannot accept on-disk operator keyfiles, or when the keyfile tier's secret-distribution and rotation burden materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
325	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260609-federation-canon.md` (*the canon* — the authoritative federation shape: two-tier plan, curl-native two-leg device-flow + STS exchange, 12-hour session cap, per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, the fully-secret-free human-present posture, and the regime/accessor homes; folds in the former source document `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`);
326	`Memos/memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1/R2/R3 credential model and three-tier ladder, plus R4/R5 test-rig and gauntlet resolutions);
327	`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (impersonation mechanism detail — its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2);
328	Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).
329

[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
==== Horizon Roadmap

Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).

Items here are not planned for any heat.
When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.

**SA numeric ID for member references.**
Google recommends using a
{giam_service_account}'s
numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
backoff profile) is necessary.
Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(SA propagation section, evidence table).

**Read-back verification universalization.**
Currently only
{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
and
{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(Convergence Analysis section).

**SA preflight universalization.**
Currently only
{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
{giam_service_account}.
Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
_Reference_:
<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
(Convergence Analysis section).

**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).

**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).

**Substrate Lode consumption — wsl/podvm host-side election and provisioning.**
Lode capture is landed: per-kind capture verbs (`ensconce`/`conclave`/`underpin`/`immure` — `enshrine` retired) each emit one provenance-enveloped GAR package, the **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_ark} on the built side and identified by its touchmark;
intent stays distributed per kind — no unified recipe noun, no resolved-FQIN field — generalizing RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern.
What remains is the consumption half for the substrate kinds: the workload's `wsl --import` consuming a verified rootfs Lode in place of the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on, and podman machine provisioning consuming a podvm Lode in place of quay's rapidly-churning upstream.
Substrate touchmarks are Director-pinned at the nameplate and materialized at charge — the Retriever receives versions, never elects them.
Open: which host-tier regime backs node-side election/provisioning (station regime vs BURN node profile vs a new host-config regime).
_Trigger_: when the Windows host workload moves onto the project-controlled WSL distro, or when podman machine provisioning must survive quay retention.
_Reference_: RBSLU-lode_underpin.adoc, RBSLI-lode_immure.adoc (the substrate capture specs);
RBSDY-director_yoke.adoc (build-time election precedent);
BUSJCW CaparisonWindows, BUSJGW GarrisonWsl (the `wsl --export` seed path this retires).

**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
_Reference_: prior design conversation capture (paddock).

**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
A
{rbtgi_depot}
holding alongside the
{rbtga_ark}
that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
+
Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
{at_bottle_container}
at charge time, rather than baking models into
{at_bottle_image_s}.
Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
{at_nameplate}-declared
hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
{at_bottle_container}
against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
This is intentional: the spec defines
{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
The DNS allowlist
({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
provides name-level precision;
the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
+
The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
+
Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
+
. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
+
_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
the tadmor nameplate's {rbrn_regime} assignment file (current CIDR configuration).

**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).
NOTE: WireGuard-in-sentry is **rejected** by the current shaping — `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` §11 moves the tunnel's local end out of the sentry (whose containment-harness purpose it would invert) into a dedicated terminus vessel, the local end of the *Outpost* (`raise`/`strike`); the reach object's current vocabulary and staging live in that memo.

**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode); current shaping `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` (the *Quire* — allowlist MVP, Outpost fast-follow, consume-side governance).

**Bottle credential custody.**
Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.
This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.
Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table); the Bedrock instance of this pattern is the Quire's confined-credential identity layer (`Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` §6).

NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.

**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.

**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
{rbtgi_depot}
creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
design conversation pending heat.

**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
A
{giam_service_account}
in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
bullions are the new AWS-side holding with S3-native retention semantics.
Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.

**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
Director holds no AWS credentials;
failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry.

**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
{at_bottle_container}
over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
NOTE: the native-consume half of this entry is renamed and currently shaped as the **Quire** in `Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md` (vocabulary, privacy boundaries, consume-side governance: Precentor/Chorister/Prebend/Cloister, deployment ladder); the custom-model-import half stays with the embassy/bullion delivery path above and remains unshaped.

**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED.**
Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
Operation-level routing: Lode capture uses tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
Lodes (builder tools and base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).

**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
Cross-reference correction (`Memos/memo-20260609-bedrock-quire-shaping.md`): the conduit/Outpost VPC is an *AWS* network and VPC-SC is a *GCP* service perimeter around the depot project — different clouds; neither can serve the other. The two items are independent.

**Cosign image signing.**
Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).

**Lode vouch signing — confirm GAR referrers, decide cosign-vs-referrers.**
The Lode provenance envelope rides today as a reserved `:rbi_vouch` tag (one per Lode), schema-versioned with `signature: null` — a clean upgrade seam reserved without spending now.
When signing becomes warranted, confirm whether GAR's OCI 1.1 referrers API is mature enough to attach attestation as a subject-linked artifact, and decide between that and cosign for the Lode vouch.
Verifying GAR referrers maturity is a vendor (Palisade) question with no consumer until distribution leaves the project, so it is deliberately not a pace.
_Trigger_: same as Cosign — external image distribution or builder strategy beyond Google Cloud Build.
_Reference_: RBS0-SpecTop.adoc (Lode provenance envelope, `rbld-vouch-2`).

**Binary Authorization.**
Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed container platforms.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).

**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in vessel build contexts are not validated.
A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04` uses a mutable tag.
Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).

**Reproducibility verification.**
Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.
Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).

**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
Recipe Bottle grants
{rbtgi_depot}
capabilities to identities the depot's IAM recognizes.
The shipping model is the keyfile tier: each operator role holds a service-account key file (the
{rbtgi_rbra_file})
on disk, possession of the key is the identity, and no GCP organization is required.
Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
The depot owner configures the
{rbtgi_depot}
to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
The
{rbtgi_depot}
remains the home of the participant roster (the role-to-identity mapping); the IdP owns only identity proofing.
Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
{rbtr_payor}
cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
A qualifying organization is free via Cloud Identity once its owner verifies control of a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.
The Google-side credential path is curl-native — an STS token exchange (RFC 8693), no gcloud and no added stack dependency; only the IdP-auth leg (device flow where the issuer supports it) varies by issuer.
Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
{at_operator}
*access* identity arriving the other way.
_Trigger_: first concrete need from an organization that cannot accept on-disk operator keyfiles, or when the keyfile tier's secret-distribution and rotation burden materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260609-federation-canon.md` (*the canon* — the authoritative federation shape: two-tier plan, curl-native two-leg device-flow + STS exchange, 12-hour session cap, per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, the fully-secret-free human-present posture, and the regime/accessor homes; folds in the former source document `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`);
`Memos/memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1/R2/R3 credential model and three-tier ladder, plus R4/R5 test-rig and gauntlet resolutions);
`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (impersonation mechanism detail — its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2);
Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).

input.old_string: **Binary Authorization.**
input.new_string:
**Progress-aware convergence deadline for cloud-side package deletes.**
`converge_delete` (rbgjl06-package-delete.py) enforces a fixed 180s per-package ceiling measured from the package's start.
`fire_delete` is synchronous HTTP, so a ≥1000-version index web can spend the entire ceiling inside round 1 — still making monotonic progress — and die mid-convergence;
the fixed shape penalizes exactly the packages that most need convergence.
Repair shape per the memo: die only on consecutive no-progress rounds (remaining-version count supplies the signal), retain a work-scaled absolute ceiling as backstop, and document the abjure-size budget against `RBRR_GCB_TIMEOUT` (per-package ceilings must sum inside the build timeout).
Latent today — the largest observed web is 55 versions, far from the edge.
_Trigger_: captured webs an order of magnitude past today's, or a convergence death attributed to the fixed ceiling.
_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-convergence-deadline-shape.md`.

**Binary Authorization.**

Triage complete — all 15 memos carry recorded dispositions: nine paces slated (₢BHAAg through ₢BHAAo), two RBSHR entries committed (078c1da0e, affiliated ₢BHAAd), and the declines are recorded in this triage (immure items 3/4/5 stand as records, the durations extraction stands down — the memo itself is the manifest — and the quoin-minting memo stays as a reference doc). I'm keeping ₢BHAAd unwrapped until heat-end since tomorrow's audit may produce straggler memos.

The distilled questions — all yes/no, my recommendation first:

1. **Kick the ladder (₢BHAAf — complete + skirmish) tonight?** Recommend yes. On your yes I'll reslate the inversion note into the ladder and ₢BHAAR dockets so the order change is a record, not drift.
2. **How much before the ladder tonight?** Only ₢BHAAg (suite-subset, tiny) is *required* so skirmish runs the corrected subset. If you have stamina, ₢BHAAh/₢BHAAi/₢BHAAj (delete-hardening, immure-residue, elect-anchor) also ride tonight's ladder for free; if not, they verify fine later. Recommend: AAg for certain, the others only if you're still upright.
3. **₢BHAAc (BURV check) tonight right after the suite lands?** Minutes of work, and it wants the fresh trace before reaping. Recommend yes.

Everything else (₢BHAAk–₢BHAAo, yoke rename, scrub, my audit) is clean morning work — none of it loses verification coverage by following the ladder. Answer with three words if you like (e.g. "yes / AAg only / yes") and I'll prepare the paste-ready prompts for the worker.

message.content: The killer is if paces need long running tests.  The opportunity is if there are paoces we just created that can run in parallel Opus (or even Fable) execution paces.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Good instinct — I checked both axes. Short version: **none of the nine new paces needs a long test** (all gate on build/shellcheck/fast — minutes; the two with live-tier needs, ₢BHAAh and ₢BHAAo, explicitly delegate that coverage). The real killer is subtler: **fixture runs serialize globally, across officia** — so once the ladder launches, *nothing* else can run even `fast` until morning. Landing code overnight without its fast gate is exactly the landed-but-never-tested failure mode. So the rule for tonight is: whatever we parallelize must **gate and wrap before the ladder kicks off**; everything else waits for morning untouched, not half-landed.

The opportunity is real, because the nine slot into file-disjoint lanes:

- **Lane 1 — current worker window** (after ₢BHAAX wraps): ₢BHAAh + ₢BHAAj + ₢BHAAk sequentially — one foundry/governor bash cluster (rbfc0/rbfld/rbgjl06, rbfd, rbgg). Bonus I hadn't surfaced: skirmish runs `canonical-invest`, so if ₢BHAAk lands tonight, the ladder live-exercises the new read-back gate for free.
- **Lane 2 — fresh Opus window**: ₢BHAAg + ₢BHAAm — both pure rbtd-crate edits (suite registry, cupel), one build, disjoint from lane 1. ₢BHAAg is the must-have.
- **Lane 3 — me**: ₢BHAAn (spec-only quoin surgery, fast-gate only, touches nothing the lanes touch). I'll take it while coordinating, since you've opened the door to Fable execution.

Held for morning: ₢BHAAo (conflicts with nothing tonight, but its opus-grade review deserves fresh eyes and it shares tomorrow's service run with ₢BHAAR), ₢BHAAl (cross-cuts theurge + credential path — too entangled for a sleepy parallel lane), yoke rename, scrub, the audit.

Coordination costs to respect: fast gates queue one at a time (cheap — minutes each, I'll sequence them), and **wraps serialize** since wrap sweeps the whole tree — each lane notches with explicit file lists, then wraps one at a time on my call. ₢BHAAc (BURV) is the one thing safe *during* the ladder — it's read-only trace inspection, no fixtures — so it can be anyone's nightcap.

If that shape works: say go and I'll write two paste-ready prompts (lane 1 for your current worker, lane 2 for a fresh window) and start ₢BHAAn myself. When the lanes report green, I sequence the wraps, reslate the inversion notes, and you launch the ladder and sleep.

## Character
The heat's batched cloud-spend verification — one ladder pass discharging every deferred live gate.
Operator-gated spend; everything before it in the rail is statically green by construction.
Order inversion cinched at triage (2026-06-10): this ladder runs the night the triage-slated mechanical fixes land,
AHEAD of the heat's remaining host-side / spec / test-infra paces
(the reproducibility audit, the credless-by-construction guard, the cupel python walk, the yoke colophon move, the vocab scrub) —
those verify at fast/static tier and gain nothing from this spend;
any of them that turns out to warrant live proof shares the next service-tier run, never a rerun of this ladder.

## Goal
Discharge the deferred live gates in one batch on the standing depot:
the skopeo-eviction bind-mode verify (delegated here by that pace's docket)
plus live coverage of the triage-slated fixes that landed ahead of it
(the cloud-delete hardening rides the complete leg's banish/abjure traffic;
the invest read-back gate rides skirmish's canonical-invest).

## Cinched
- skirmish, not gauntlet — same bind-leg coverage, no project churn;
  fresh-project coverage belongs to the operator's standing depot-recreate plan, not this heat.
- `complete` rides first (service ∪ crucible — the cheap non-ladder tier), then skirmish.
- This run is also the durations re-baseline (triage 2026-06-10 confirmed the disposition):

  may have no post-cutover live verification — check the heat's run record at mount;

# jjezs_reslate BHAAR
## Character
Reproducibility-correctness audit across every image construction and acquisition path — design and judgment, not mechanical prose.
The original "teach the onboarding clean-tree convention" framing is subsumed: a premise it rested on — that the clean-tree gate is already uniform across the Director tracks — proved false at slate-time, so the real work is making the guarantee certain in code first, then teaching only what the code enforces.
**MUST be worked by Fable (claude-fable-5).** Operator-required tier; do not delegate the audit judgment to a cheaper model.

## Order note (triage 2026-06-10)
The endgame verification ladder runs before this pace by operator cinch —
do not expect ladder coverage for the gates this audit lands.
They verify at fast/static tier (a dirty-tree refusal is honestly testable without cloud);
any change here that genuinely warrants live proof shares the next service-tier run
(verification batching), never a ladder rerun.
Rider from the eviction pace wrap: the 2026-06-10 conclave run incidentally live-verified the capture-side gate.

## Governing rule
Every image RB constructs or acquires must trace to a clean git commit, or it is not reproducible.
Make that certain: every path that builds or acquires an image refuses on a dirty working tree — before any step stamps git provenance or ships bytes to GAR — and closes any gap by refusing, never by auto-committing.

## Starting map — confirm, do not trust
The gate is not uniform today.
`bug_require_clean_tree` has only two callers — kludge and bind-mode mirror.
Conjure- and graft-mode `rbfd_ordain` do not gate; the conjure base-anchor election (`zrbfd_elect_base_anchor`) writes rbrv.env ungated; inscribe (reliquary, depot tripwire) does not gate despite the mirror guard's "same as inscribe" comment; the Lode capture verbs write GAR but their clean-tree story is unverified; the only near-universal refusal is the path-scoped `rbob_charge` nameplate gate.
Re-derive this map from scratch — line-level specifics drift between slate and mount.

## Discovery recipe — locate every image-construction or -acquisition path
- Made side: `rbfd_ordain` (conjure / bind / graft modes), `rbfk_kludge`, the cloud `builds.create` submission, and `zrbfc_ensure_git_metadata` — the chokepoint that stamps the git commit into provenance, so anything reaching it on a dirty tree records a commit that does not match the bytes built.
- Fetched side / Lode processing: every capture verb (`ensconce`/`conclave`/`underpin`/`immure`) and the shared spine `rblds_Spine.sh` — decide whether a captured Lode's reproducibility depends on RB's tree at all, and whether a dirty tree can corrupt its provenance envelope.
- For each path, classify: gates-today / must-gate / correctly-ungated-with-stated-reason (graft's "image already built, git state irrelevant" is the model for the last).

## Cinched
- The invariant is reproducibility, not ergonomics — a path that can emit or acquire an image from a dirty tree is the bug, however rarely it is hit.
- Tools never commit; the fix is always to refuse, and Marshal-Zero stays the sole sanctioned self-commit.
- Onboarding prose follows the code: teach "commit before <step>" only at the tracks that actually gate once this audit lands, never a fiction.

## Done when
Every image-construction and -acquisition path, made and fetched, is accounted for — each either refuses on a dirty tree before the act, or carries a stated, defensible reason it need not.
The guarantee "every RB image traces to a clean commit" is certain across both sides, and the onboarding convention is surfaced only where the code now enforces it.

Rail is committed and the inversion is recorded in both dockets. Here's the picture, as simple as I can make it:

## Railed order

**Tonight:** ₢BHAAg → ₢BHAAm → ₢BHAAh → ₢BHAAj → ₢BHAAk → ₢BHAAi → ₢BHAAn → ₢BHAAo (stretch) → ₢BHAAc → **₢BHAAf (overnight ladder)**
**Morning:** ₢BHAAR → ₢BHAAl → ₢BHAAb → ₢BHAAD → ₢BHAAd (triage close)

## Parallel chats — **I recommend 3**

| Chat | Paces | Files it owns |
|------|-------|---------------|
| **Chat 1** (your current window, post-/clear) | ₢BHAAg then ₢BHAAm | theurge crate (suite registry, cupel) + CLAUDE.md |
| **Chat 2** (new) | ₢BHAAh → ₢BHAAj → ₢BHAAk | foundry/governor bash + rbgjl06 + RBSCB |
| **Chat 3** (new) | ₢BHAAi (then ₢BHAAo only if you're still upright) | rbgjl07/08/09 + acronyms doc |
| **Me** | ₢BHAAn now, ₢BHAAc after | specs only / trace dirs only |

Three because the lanes are fully file-disjoint and the longest pole drops from four paces to three — and with me running ₢BHAAn, you're shepherding three windows either way. If three feels heavy, fold chat 3's ₢BHAAi onto the end of chat 2 and accept the later ladder start.

## The one rule that makes parallel safe

Tell each chat (I'd put it in the mount message verbatim): **"Code, shellcheck, build — but do NOT run `fast` or any fixture, do NOT wrap. Notch with explicit file lists, then report ready."** Fixtures serialize globally, so when all lanes report, **I run one consolidated build + fast covering everything**, then you say "wrap" in each chat in rail order. Then chat 1 mounts ₢BHAAf, kicks `complete` then skirmish, and you go to bed — it reports in the morning.

Starting ₢BHAAn myself now.

# jjezs_reslate BHAAg
## Character
Mechanical registry edit; membership is compile-checked.
Tier: sonnet-delegable.
Must land before the endgame ladder runs — the overnight skirmish should run the corrected subset.

## Tonight's parallel-lane protocol (operator-cinched 2026-06-10)
This pace runs in parallel-lane Chat 1; other lanes are editing disjoint files concurrently.
- Code, shellcheck, and the theurge build (`tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh`) are allowed.
- Do NOT run `fast`, any `rbw-ts`/`rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc`, or any fixture — fixture runs serialize globally;
  one consolidated build+fast gate runs centrally after all lanes report.
- Notch via jjx_record with an explicit file list when code-complete. Do NOT wrap —
  wrap sweeps the whole tree; the operator triggers each wrap after the central gate is green.
- Touch only this pace's files; other lanes' uncommitted work is not yours.
- When notched: report ready, then proceed directly to ₢BHAAm in this same session (same crate).

## Goal
Repair the hand-copied fast subset embedded in the gauntlet and skirmish suites,
and refresh the stale CLAUDE.md test-execution table.

## Done when
- gauntlet and skirmish in RBTDRC_SUITES (rbtdrc_crucible.rs) carry the full fast-fixture set
  (both currently miss handbook-render, foundry-path, recipe-validation).
- The CLAUDE.md test-execution table matches the registry composition
  and names the five release/probe suites it currently omits.
- Build green; fast green via the central consolidated gate.

## Sources
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-lode-operation-durations.md (suite registry inventory section).

# jjezs_reslate BHAAm
## Character
Test-infrastructure extension; no cloud surface, no ladder dependency.
Tier: sonnet-delegable.

## Tonight's parallel-lane protocol (operator-cinched 2026-06-10)
Second pace of parallel-lane Chat 1, mounted in the same session as the suite-subset repair.
- Code, shellcheck, and the theurge build (`tt/rbw-tb.Build.sh`) are allowed.
- Do NOT run `fast`, any `rbw-ts`/`rbw-tf`/`rbw-tc`, or any fixture — one consolidated
  build+fast gate runs centrally after all lanes report.
- Notch via jjx_record with an explicit file list when code-complete. Do NOT wrap.
- Touch only this pace's files; other lanes' uncommitted work is not yours.
- When notched: report lane done and STOP.

## Goal
Extend the cupel supply-chain conformance to *.py cloud steps
(it walks *.sh only today; the python steps are entirely unscanned —
the live specimen is rbgjv02 subprocess-running gcloud invisibly):
- import allowlist anchored on the module root of every import;
  stdlib floor per the memo; importlib / __import__ / exec / eval banned outright.
- subprocess argv[0] literals scanned against ZRBTDRU_GCB_ALLOWED —
  one tool floor, two languages; adjudicate rbgjv02's gcloud by allowlisting it
  with a dated comment (no REST conversion this pace).
- ten-minute probe: is zrbfc_expand_includes language-blind
  (the #@rbgjs_include marker is a valid python comment)?
  Record the answer against CBG CBp_101 (the preamble-duplication gap).

## Done when
The cupel walks python step files; an unsanctioned import or subprocess target fails
the cupel fixture; fast green via the central consolidated gate.

## Sources
Memos/memo-20260610-heat-BH-fable-recommendation-python-import-allowlist.md
(carries the empirical import floor and the CBG addenda, including the
authoritative-floor-lives-on-the-constant decision).

# jjezs_reslate BHAAh
## Character
Four small repairs on one delete surface, bundled for one review and one live gate
(the endgame ladder's banish/abjure traffic covers them).
Tier: sonnet-delegable.

## Goal
- Pin ZRBFC_DELETE_BUILDER by digest (rbfc0_FoundryCore.sh) — it is the only floating
  builder running under a delete-privileged identity (Director SA);
  record the pin in RBSCB's cloud-dispatch posture section.

  "Jettisoned or nonexistent" message; idempotent delete is the house shape.
- rbgjl06-package-delete.py fire_delete: catch URLError alongside HTTPError
  (HTTPError subclasses URLError — structure the handler accordingly),

All seven dockets now carry their lane protocol — each chat will read its marching orders at mount. Launch is now just this:

**Window 1:** `/clear` then **`mount ₢BHAAg`** — it will do ₢BHAAg then ₢BHAAm itself.
**Window 2:** `/clear` then **`mount ₢BHAAh`** — it will chain ₢BHAAj and ₢BHAAk itself.

**Window 4:** this one — I take ₢BHAAn and ₢BHAAc while your lanes run.

Each mount will propose its approach and ask to proceed — just say go. When a lane says "lane done," nothing for you to do; when **all three** have reported done, come back here and I'll run the single consolidated build+fast gate, then walk you through wraps in rail order (one word per window), then Window 1 mounts ₢BHAAf and you launch the overnight ladder and sleep.

content.content:
1	//axhob_operation
2	{rbtgo_image_audit}
3	
4	NOTE: This operation produces a flat catalog of identifiers under one of the two GAR domains
5	(hallmarks, enshrinements)
6	in
7	{rbtgi_repository}
8	via the GAR REST API implemented by
9	{gar_service}.
10	Identifiers only — no per-member presence detail (use {rbtgo_image_rekon} for that), and no health classification (use {rbtgo_hallmark_tally} for that).
11	Reliquary cohorts are no longer audited here — a reliquary is now a conclave {rbtga_lode}, enumerated by {rbtgo_lode_divine}.
12	
13	The
14	{rbtr_director}
15	audits over two domains, each backed by a distinct procedure:
16	
17	* `rbfl_audit_hallmarks` — every hallmark identifier under the `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/` root
18	* `rbfl_audit_enshrinements` — every enshrined base anchor under the `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/` root
19	
20	Audit vs. {rbtgo_hallmark_tally}: audit is a {rbtr_director}-tier flat-list operation across all three domains; tally is a {rbtr_retriever}-tier hallmarks-only operation that classifies each entry by basename completeness and vouch status.
21	The two roles compose without overlap.
22	
23	== Hallmarks Audit
24	
25	The
26	{rbtr_director}
27	audits hallmark identifiers:
28	
29	//axhos_step
30	. *Authenticate as
31	{rbtr_director}*:
32	.. Execute
33	{rbtoe_director_authenticate}
34	.. {rbbc_store}
35	`«DIRECTOR_TOKEN»` for subsequent API calls
36	
37	//axhos_step
38	. *Enumerate Packages Under Hallmarks Root*:
39	.. {rbbc_store}
40	`«HALLMARKS_ROOT»` from `RBGL_HALLMARKS_ROOT` (kindled from `RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_HALLMARKS`, the token `rbi_hm`)
41	.. {rbbc_store}
42	`«REPO_BASE»` as the GAR REST API repository base (`projects/«PROJECT»/locations/«REGION»/repositories/«REPOSITORY»`)
43	.. {rbbc_call}
44	link:https://cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.repositories.packages/list[GAR REST API: packages.list] with:
45	... method: `GET`
46	... endpoint: `«REPO_BASE»/packages?pageSize=1000`
47	... header: `Authorization: Bearer «DIRECTOR_TOKEN»`
48	.. {rbbc_require}
49	HTTP 200 response
50	.. Extract `.packages[].name`, strip the resource prefix, URL-decode `%2F` back to `/`, and retain only entries beginning with `«HALLMARKS_ROOT»/`
51	.. {rbbc_store}
52	`«HALLMARK_ROWS»` as `«HALLMARK» «BASENAME»` pairs derived from those entries (a 2-deep enumeration shared with {rbtgo_image_rekon})
53	
54	//axhos_step
55	. *Project to Unique Identifiers*:
56	.. {rbbc_store}
57	`«HALLMARKS»` as the ordered set of distinct hallmark identifiers from the first column of `«HALLMARK_ROWS»`
58	
59	//axhoot_typed_output
60	{rbtga_ark}
61	. *Display Results*:
62	.. If `«HALLMARKS»` is empty:
63	... {rbbc_show}
64	"No hallmarks found under «HALLMARKS_ROOT»/"
65	... return success with count `0`
66	.. Otherwise:
67	... {rbbc_show}
68	table header `HALLMARK`
69	... For each `«HALLMARK»` in `«HALLMARKS»`:
70	.... {rbbc_show}
71	`«HALLMARK»`
72	... {rbbc_show}
73	"Total hallmarks: «COUNT»"
74	
75	== Enshrinements Audit
76	
77	The
78	{rbtr_director}
79	audits enshrined base anchors:
80	
81	//axhos_step
82	. *Authenticate as
83	{rbtr_director}*:
84	.. Execute
85	{rbtoe_director_authenticate}
86	.. {rbbc_store}
87	`«DIRECTOR_TOKEN»` for subsequent API calls
88	
89	//axhos_step
90	. *Enumerate Anchors Under Enshrines Root*:
91	.. {rbbc_store}
92	`«ENSHRINES_ROOT»` from `RBGL_ENSHRINES_ROOT` (kindled from `RBGC_GAR_CATEGORY_ENSHRINES`, the token `rbi_es`)
93	.. {rbbc_store}
94	`«REPO_BASE»` as the GAR REST API repository base
95	.. {rbbc_call}
96	link:https://cloud.google.com/artifact-registry/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects.locations.repositories.packages/list[GAR REST API: packages.list] with:
97	... method: `GET`
98	... endpoint: `«REPO_BASE»/packages?pageSize=1000`
99	... header: `Authorization: Bearer «DIRECTOR_TOKEN»`
100	.. {rbbc_require}
101	HTTP 200 response
102	.. Extract `.packages[].name`, strip the resource prefix, URL-decode `%2F` back to `/`, and retain only entries beginning with `«ENSHRINES_ROOT»/`
103	.. {rbbc_store}
104	`«ANCHORS»` as the list of anchor segments (1-deep enumeration — enshrines are flat, one image per anchor; see helper `zrbfc_list_anchors_capture`)
105	
106	//axhoot_typed_output
107	{rbtga_ark}
108	. *Display Results*:
109	.. If `«ANCHORS»` is empty:
110	... {rbbc_show}
111	"No enshrinements found under «ENSHRINES_ROOT»/"
112	... return success with count `0`
113	.. Otherwise:
114	... {rbbc_show}
115	table header `ENSHRINE-ANCHOR`
116	... For each `«ANCHOR»` in `«ANCHORS»`:
117	.... {rbbc_show}
118	`«ANCHOR»`
119	... {rbbc_show}
120	"Total enshrinements: «COUNT»"
121	
122	//axhoc_completion
123	Each procedure completes successfully when the ordered list of identifiers — possibly empty — has been displayed and the count line emitted.
124	The empty case is non-fatal: an empty domain reports zero rather than failing, since "registry holds no entries in this domain" is a valid director observation, not an error.
125

{gar_hallmarks_namespace}::
GAR top-level path token under which every {rbtga_hallmark}'s ark packages live as basename siblings (`image`, `about`, `vouch`, `diags`, `attest`, `pouch`).
A path-segment value, not a cloud-visible resource name; carries no {rbrd_cloud_prefix} since each {rbtgi_depot} owns its own GAR repo.

[[gar_reliquaries_namespace]]

GAR top-level path token under which reliquary tool images live as basename siblings, keyed by {rbst_reliquary_stamp}.

[[gar_enshrines_namespace]]
{gar_enshrines_namespace}::
GAR top-level path token under which enshrined upstream base images live as peer packages keyed by content-addressed anchor.
A path-segment value, not a cloud-visible resource name; carries no {rbrd_cloud_prefix} since each {rbtgi_depot} owns its own GAR repo.

3870	//axl_voices axtu_string
3871	{rbst_hallmark}::
3872	Dual-timestamp {rbtga_hallmark} identifying a specific build of a vessel.
3873	Format: `«MODE»YYMMDDHHMMSS-rYYMMDDHHMMSS` where `«MODE»` is `c` (conjure), `b` (bind), or `g` (graft).
3874	Example: `c260224153022-r260224160530`; 1-128 characters.
3875	Regex: `[cbg]\d{12}-r\d{12}`.
3876	
3877	[[rbst_reliquary_stamp]]
3878	//axl_voices axtu_string
3879	{rbst_reliquary_stamp}::
3880	Touchmark identifying a reliquary — the single GAR package holding the co-versioned build-tool cohort for builder use.
3881	Format: `r` + `YYMMDDHHMMSS` (e.g., `r260327172456`); 13 characters.
3882	Regex: `r\d{12}`.
3883	Mints host-side at {rbtgo_lode_conclave} time and serves as the GAR path segment for the conclave {rbtga_lode} (`{gar_lodes_namespace}/«TOUCHMARK»`); the cohort members ride within that one package as `:rbi_<tool>` member tags rather than per-tool sub-packages, so a single value names the whole package and the sprued tags pin every member.
3884	
3885	[[rbst_touchmark]]
3886	//axl_voices axtu_string
3887	{rbst_touchmark}::
3888	Datestamp identifying a {rbtga_lode} — the GAR package holding a captured upstream artifact and its provenance.
3889	Format: `«KIND»YYMMDDHHMMSS`, where `«KIND»` is the kind-letter (`b` bole, `t` tool, `r` reliquary, `w` wsl, `vw`/`vn` podvm) and the datestamp is second-granular (e.g., `b260602120000`).
3890	Regex: `[a-z]{1,2}\d{12}`.
3891	Mints host-side at {rbtgo_lode_ensconce} time and serves as the GAR path segment (`{gar_lodes_namespace}/«TOUCHMARK»`), so a single value names the whole Lode package.
3892	The fetched-side parallel of {rbst_hallmark}; carried as the concept {rbtga_touchmark}.
3893

//axl_voices axtu_string
{rbst_hallmark}::
Dual-timestamp {rbtga_hallmark} identifying a specific build of a vessel.
Format: `«MODE»YYMMDDHHMMSS-rYYMMDDHHMMSS` where `«MODE»` is `c` (conjure), `b` (bind), or `g` (graft).
Example: `c260224153022-r260224160530`; 1-128 characters.
Regex: `[cbg]\d{12}-r\d{12}`.

[[rbst_reliquary_stamp]]
//axl_voices axtu_string
{rbst_reliquary_stamp}::
Touchmark identifying a reliquary — the single GAR package holding the co-versioned build-tool cohort for builder use.
Format: `r` + `YYMMDDHHMMSS` (e.g., `r260327172456`); 13 characters.
Regex: `r\d{12}`.
Mints host-side at {rbtgo_lode_conclave} time and serves as the GAR path segment for the conclave {rbtga_lode} (`{gar_lodes_namespace}/«TOUCHMARK»`); the cohort members ride within that one package as `:rbi_<tool>` member tags rather than per-tool sub-packages, so a single value names the whole package and the sprued tags pin every member.

[[rbst_touchmark]]
//axl_voices axtu_string
{rbst_touchmark}::
Datestamp identifying a {rbtga_lode} — the GAR package holding a captured upstream artifact and its provenance.
Format: `«KIND»YYMMDDHHMMSS`, where `«KIND»` is the kind-letter (`b` bole, `t` tool, `r` reliquary, `w` wsl, `vw`/`vn` podvm) and the datestamp is second-granular (e.g., `b260602120000`).
Regex: `[a-z]{1,2}\d{12}`.
Mints host-side at {rbtgo_lode_ensconce} time and serves as the GAR path segment (`{gar_lodes_namespace}/«TOUCHMARK»`), so a single value names the whole Lode package.
The fetched-side parallel of {rbst_hallmark}; carried as the concept {rbtga_touchmark}.

[[rbst_path]]
