content.content:
13:It absorbs the legacy inscribe tool-mirror — retargeted from the per-tool sibling-package layout to N member tags within one Lode package, plus the unifying
90:... Copy the upstream image registry->registry with `gcrane --platform linux/amd64 cp` (daemonless — no docker pull/tag/push; the single amd64 manifest only) into the one Lode package under the clean member tag `:rbi_<tool>` — a distinct manifest per tool, all sharing the one package; no digest or fingerprint layer
100:.. For the staged envelope, build a `FROM scratch` image copying `vouch.json` and push it to the Lode package under the `:rbi_vouch` tag — a distinct manifest sharing the one package
116:— carrying no provenance content; the envelope is the in-GAR `:rbi_vouch` tag, written cloud-side by the vouch step

input.old_string:
[[rbst_reliquary_stamp]]
//axl_voices axtu_string
{rbst_reliquary_stamp}::
Touchmark identifying a reliquary — the single GAR package holding the co-versioned build-tool cohort for builder use.
Format: `r` + `YYMMDDHHMMSS` (e.g., `r260327172456`); 13 characters.
Regex: `r\d{12}`.
Mints host-side at {rbtgo_lode_conclave} time and serves as the GAR path segment for the conclave {rbtga_lode} (`{gar_lodes_namespace}/«TOUCHMARK»`); the cohort members ride within that one package as `:rbi_<tool>` member tags rather than per-tool sub-packages, so a single value names the whole package and the sprued tags pin every member.

NOTE: This operation produces a flat catalog of identifiers under one of the two GAR domains
(hallmarks, enshrinements)
in
{rbtgi_repository}
via the GAR REST API implemented by
{gar_service}.
Identifiers only — no per-member presence detail (use {rbtgo_image_rekon} for that), and no health classification (use {rbtgo_hallmark_tally} for that).
Reliquary cohorts are no longer audited here — a reliquary is now a conclave {rbtga_lode}, enumerated by {rbtgo_lode_divine}.

The
{rbtr_director}
audits over two domains, each backed by a distinct procedure:

* `rbfl_audit_hallmarks` — every hallmark identifier under the `{gar_hallmarks_namespace}/` root
* `rbfl_audit_enshrinements` — every enshrined base anchor under the `{gar_enshrines_namespace}/` root

Audit vs. {rbtgo_hallmark_tally}: audit is a {rbtr_director}-tier flat-list operation across all three domains; tally is a {rbtr_retriever}-tier hallmarks-only operation that classifies each entry by basename completeness and vouch status.
The two roles compose without overlap.

6	
7	# Bash Console Guide (BCG) - Pattern Reference
8	
9	## Core Philosophy
10	
11	This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
12	
13	The aesthetic of elegant bash pipelines trades debuggability for brevity. A failing command in `$(echo | sed | awk)` leaves no trace. Temp files make each transformation step visible, testable, and recoverable.
14	
15	Traps and signal handlers create action-at-a-distance effects that obscure control flow. When error handling lives at the point of failure rather than in distant handlers, code review and maintenance become predictable.
16	
17	Enterprise bash isn't about looking clever; it's about being boringly, reliably correct. These patterns optimize for the 3am debugging session, not the initial writing experience. Each external process spawn is a potential failure point, so bash builtins are preferred despite verbosity.
18	
19	The patterns reject bash 4+ features to maintain compatibility with older enterprise systems still running bash 3.2, particularly older RHEL/CentOS or macOS deployments.
20	
21	### Load-Bearing Complexity
22	
23	Every element in a system — every function extraction, every pattern variant, every structural distinction — must carry weight. An element is **load-bearing** when its removal would create a gap between intent and behavior. When similar things differ, ask whether the difference is load-bearing: if yes, document why; if no, homogenize.
24	
25	This is the general decision criterion that tools like Zeroes Theory instantiate. Zeroes Theory asks "how many zeroes did this add to state space?" — load-bearing complexity asks the prior question: "does this element earn its existence at all?"
26	
27	Concrete examples from this codebase:
28	
29	- **Four separate `rbgi_add_*_iam` functions** look like copy-paste, but each wraps a different GCP API with different failure modes. The variation is load-bearing — extracting a "generic IAM helper" would hide which API failed at 3am.
30	- **Three copy-pasted Secret Manager IAM blocks** are NOT load-bearing variation — same API, same failure class, same recovery. Extract a helper.
31	- **Not adding three-part structure to a simple `api_enable` spec definition** — the structure would add ceremony without carrying meaning. Non-load-bearing elements increase cognitive cost without increasing correctness.
32	
33	Non-load-bearing elements are the silent budget overruns of a codebase. Each one is small; their aggregate is what makes systems incomprehensible.
34	
35	The allocation instance of this principle — *reference the home, don't recreate* (values → constants, concepts → quoin-refs) — is its own language-neutral guide: see **ACG** (`Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/ACG-AllocationCodingGuide.md`).
36	
37	## Module Architecture
38	
39	Every module has an implementation file. CLI entry points are only present if module requires standalone execution.
40	
41	```bash
42	«prefix»_«name».sh (implementation - REQUIRED, prefer single lowercase word, allow snake_case)
43	«prefix»_cli.sh (entry point - OPTIONAL, omit for library/utility modules)
44	```
45	
46	When a module outgrows one implementation file, it decomposes across several files while remaining one module — see **Module Decomposition** below.
47	
48	### CLI as Module Gateway
49	
50	**Rule**: Code outside the BCG module system must never call `z*_kindle()` directly. All external access to BCG modules goes through CLI scripts.
51	
52	**Pattern**:
53	```
54	External Code → CLI script → furnish() kindles → module functions
55	```
56	
57	**Why this matters**:
58	- Kindle sequences are implementation details that may change
59	- The CLI's `furnish()` owns the kindle graph (which modules to kindle, in what order)
60	- External code remains stable when module dependencies change
61	- CLIs provide the documented, stable API for module functionality
62	
63	**Anti-pattern**:
64	```bash
65	# ❌ External script kindling directly
66	source "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_bottle.sh"
67	zrbob_kindle                          # VIOLATION: kindle leaked outside CLI
68	rbob_charge
69	```
70	
71	**Correct pattern**:
72	```bash
73	# ✅ External script delegates to CLI
74	exec "${TOOLS_DIR}/rbob_cli.sh" rbob_charge "$@"
75	```
76	
77	**What counts as "external code"**:
78	- Entry point scripts (workbenches, testbenches, coordinators)
79	- TabTargets and launchers
80	- Any dispatcher or router that invokes module functionality
81	- Scripts that are not themselves BCG modules
82	
83	**What can call kindle**:
84	- A module's own CLI (in `furnish()`)
85	- Another CLI's `furnish()` (when CLI A depends on module B, A's furnish kindles B)
86	- Test harnesses that explicitly isolate module behavior
87	
88	**Filesystem marker**: The `_cli.sh` suffix and the executable bit must agree. Within a BCG module directory:
89	
90	- Every `*_cli.sh` MUST be executable
91	- Every other `*.sh` MUST NOT be executable
92	
93	This makes the gateway role visible to `ls -l`: a workbench, testbench, or implementation module carrying `+x` is a bug — direct execution bypasses the CLI's `furnish()` and the kindle graph it owns. Tabtargets in `tt/` and shipped tool binaries are not BCG modules and stand outside this rule.
94	
95	### Module-CLI Prefix Unity
96	
97	**Convention**: A module and its CLI share a prefix (`rbrn_regime.sh` + `rbrn_cli.sh`).
98	
99	When commands within a single CLI have different dependency weights (e.g., `validate` needs
100	only the regime file, `audit` needs GCP/OAuth), use **differential furnish**: the furnish
101	function receives the command name as `$1` and conditionally sources/kindles heavy
102	dependencies based on which command is being invoked.
103	
104	### Regime Module Archetype
105	
106	A **regime** is a sourced `.env` file containing typed configuration fields — the contract between human-authored configuration and the modules that consume it. When a module's purpose is validating a regime, it follows the regime archetype:
107	
108	| Component | Location | Purpose |
109	|-----------|----------|---------|
110	| `buv_*_enroll` calls | kindle | Declare field contracts (type, range, description) |
111	| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
112	| `z«prefix»_enforce` | regime module | `buv_vet SCOPE` + custom format checks |
113	| `readonly` lock | after enforce | Lock enrolled variables against mutation |
114	| `*_validate` command | CLI | `buv_report` — explained per-field display |
115	| `*_render` command | CLI | `buv_render` — diagnostic dump |
116	
117	Furnish sequence: kindle → enforce → lock (ironclad gate before any command runs). After enforce succeeds, lock all enrolled `PREFIX_*` variables with `readonly` to prevent accidental mutation by downstream code. Any derived state (e.g., docker env arrays) must be built from validated values after enforce, before or during the lock step.
118	
119	**Singleton** regimes source config unconditionally in furnish. **Manifold** regimes (multiple instances, e.g. nameplates) source conditionally when a folio identifies the instance.
120	
121	Enrollment types and gating API: see `buv_validation.sh`.
122	
123	### Module Decomposition
124	
125	Module Architecture above assumes one module fits in one implementation file. When a module outgrows that — the single file grows past comprehension, or distinct gesture-clusters within it acquire independent mass — the module **decomposes** across several files while remaining one module. Decomposition is load-bearing only when the single file genuinely no longer fits: a module that fits in one file stays in one file (Load-Bearing Complexity).
126	
127	**The container prefix names no file.** Once a module decomposes, its prefix becomes a *container* — the function namespace and the sort-root for the file family, but no file is named `«prefix»_«name».sh` with the bare prefix. Terminal exclusivity here is a **filesystem** invariant, not a function-namespace one: the dispatch functions keep the bare container prefix (`«prefix»_«command»`, `buc_execute «prefix»_ …`) even though every *file* carries a tier marker. A reader greps `«prefix»_` for behavior and `«prefix»0_`/`«prefix»«g»_` for files.
128	
129	Decomposition has two tiers.
130	
131	**Top tier — `«prefix»0_`** (the `0` sorts it to the head of an `ls`):
132	
133	| File | Role | Executable | Guard |
134	|------|------|------------|-------|
135	| `«prefix»0_cli.sh` | CLI gateway — `z«prefix»_furnish`, `buc_execute «prefix»_ …` | yes | — (CLI header) |
136	| `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` | Entry — the single inclusion-guard, the whole kindle, the sentinel | no | `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` |
137	
138	The entry is the module's **sourcing hub**: it holds the one `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED` guard and the complete `z«prefix»_kindle` / `z«prefix»_sentinel`, and it sources the cluster bodies. The CLI's furnish sources the entry (alongside the module's dependencies) and then calls `z«prefix»_kindle` — the CLI-as-gateway contract is unchanged; only the file furnish reaches is now the entry hub rather than a lone `«prefix»_«name».sh`. The entry is not the CLI: the executable gateway stays in `«prefix»0_cli.sh`.
139	
140	**Cluster tier — `«prefix»«g»_«Name».sh`** (gesture-letter bodies):
141	
142	Each cluster is a body file carrying one coherent group of the module's functions, named with a gesture letter (`«g»`) that scopes the group. Clusters are **guard-free**: the entry sources each exactly once, so a per-cluster inclusion guard would be inert ceremony. Clusters read the entry's `readonly Z«PREFIX»_*` kindle constants as globals — kindle runs in the entry, the constants are visible module-wide.
143	
144	**The single-guard rule, and its one exception.** Exactly one file carries `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED`: the entry. Clusters omit it because they are sourced once, by the entry alone. The exception is a cluster sourced by **more than one** entry — e.g. a primitives cluster shared between a module and an independent consumer that sources it directly. A multiply-sourced cluster keeps its own `Z«PREFIX»…_SOURCED` guard, because the "sourced exactly once" premise no longer holds. The guard tracks *how many entries source the file*, not the tier.
145	
146	**Sourcing.** Decomposition adds one sanctioned sourcing site beyond the three in Sourcing Rules: the entry hub sources its sibling cluster bodies (and, where a dependency is itself decomposed, that dependency's entry). This is the kindle graph made physical — see **Sourcing Rules**.
147	
148	**Filesystem marker.** The `_cli.sh`-is-executable invariant is unchanged: `«prefix»0_cli.sh` carries `+x`; the entry `«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` and every cluster do not. Decomposition adds files but no new executables.
149	
150	**Topology — flat by default.** A single decomposed module's files live flat in the kit directory alongside undecomposed modules; the `0`/gesture markers already group them in a listing. A subdirectory is reserved for a *family of distinct modules* grouped for cohesion (each with its own `«prefix»0_cli` + entry) — never for the parts of one decomposed module. One module decomposing is not grounds for a subdirectory.
151	
152	`«prefix»0_«ModuleName».sh` is the canonical entry form. An earlier interim form postfixed the entry with `k` (`«prefix»k_«Name».sh`); see **Fading Memory FM-002**.
153	
154	---
155	
156	## Function Patterns
157	
158	### Boilerplate Functions (one per module/CLI)
159	
160	| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
161	|----------------------|----------------|----------------------------------------------------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
162	| `z«prefix»_kindle`   | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | Yes (credentials) | No (use buc_log_*)| Define all kindle constants, set module state    |
163	| `z«prefix»_sentinel` | Implementation | `test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" \|\| buc_die` | No                | No                | Guard all other functions                        |
164	| `z«prefix»_enforce`  | Implementation | `z«prefix»_sentinel`                               | No                | No                | Validate sourced config (regime archetype only)  |
165	| `z«prefix»_furnish`  | CLI only       | `buc_doc_env...`                                   | Yes (all deps)    | No                | Document env vars, source all deps, kindle       |
166	| Module header        | Implementation | `test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" \|\| buc_die`    | No                | N/A               | Prevent multiple inclusion                       |
167	| CLI header           | CLI            | `set -euo pipefail`                                | Yes (buc_command)  | N/A               | Source buc_command.sh only                       |
168	
169	**Kindle constant**: Any variable — internal (`Z«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) or public (`«PREFIX»_SCREAMING_NAME`) — defined exclusively within the kindle function with `readonly`. No other function — including enroll, setup, or helper functions — may assign to kindle constants. This ensures module state is fully determined at kindle time and visible in one place.
170	
171	**Mutable kindle state**: A variable initialized in kindle but intentionally mutated after kindle returns (counters, accumulators, registry rolls, builder state). Uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name` to visually distinguish from `readonly` kindle constants. Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state.
172	
173	**Tinder constant**: A public variable (`«PREFIX»_lower_name`) defined at module top level, immediately after the `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` guard. Tinder constants must resolve to pure string literals at source time — **no runtime dependency, no `$(…)` computation, no expansion of kindle constants or runtime variables**. Tinder-on-tinder expansion is permitted: a tinder value may interpolate `${«PREFIX»_lower_name}` for any tinder constant defined earlier in the same module's tinder block, since that expansion resolves at source time and preserves the no-runtime-dependency invariant. They are available immediately after sourcing — no kindle required. Use `SCREAMING` case for the prefix (module identity) and `lower_snake` case for the name to visually distinguish from kindle constants. This enables sourcing chains where a tinder constant from module A provides the path for sourcing module B's config in the furnish function. The name reflects their role in the kindle metaphor: tinder is the fixed material prepared before kindling — inert, unchanging, and enabling the fire to start.
174	
175	**KINDLED must be last**: `readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1` must be the final statement in kindle. Since sentinel checks this variable, setting it last guarantees all kindle constants, roll arrays, and enroll calls are complete before the module is considered operational. Any function calling sentinel before kindle finishes will correctly fail. The `readonly` makes re-kindling a loud error.
176	
177	### Regular Functions (output via printouts/temp files)
178	
179	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
180	
181	| Type            | Pattern              | First Line           | Parameters | buc_step | Documentation        |
182	|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------|------------|----------|----------------------|
183	| Public          | `«prefix»_«command»` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Required (buc_doc_*) |
184	| Internal helper | `z«prefix»_«name»`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `"${1:-}"` | Yes      | Recommended (buc_doc_*) |
185	
186	---
187	
188	## File Templates
189	
190	### Template 1: Shebang and Copyright
191	
192	```bash
193	#!/bin/bash
194	#
195	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
196	#
197	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
198	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
199	# You may obtain a copy of the License at
200	#
201	#     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
202	#
203	# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
204	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
205	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
206	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
207	# limitations under the License.
208	#
209	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
210	#
211	```
212	
213	**Cross-module references need no per-file directive.** Files that reference cross-module `Z*_/RB*_` readonly state trip SC2153 (possible misspelling) and SC2154 (referenced but not assigned). Both are suppressed globally in `busc_shellcheckrc`, applied on the canonical lint path (the project's shellcheck tabtarget always passes `--rcfile`). No inline directive is carried — see **Shellcheck Integration** and **Prohibited Constructs § Inline Shellcheck Directives**.
214	
215	The `set -u` clause of `set -euo pipefail` is the runtime backstop for the same typo class SC2153 catches statically: a misspelled cross-module reference dies at first reference rather than silently expanding to empty.
216	
217	### Tabtarget Path Indirection
218	
219	A tabtarget in `tt/` is a colophon, not a program — it names *what* to run, never *how* to reach it. A single trampoline, `tt/z-launcher.sh`, owns the "how." Every other tabtarget is a declarative stub: a shebang, a `BURD_*` config block, and a byte-identical `exec` into the trampoline.
220	
221	```bash
222	#!/bin/bash
223	export BURD_LAUNCHER=launcher.«workbench»_workbench.sh
224	exec "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/z-launcher.sh" "${0##*/}" "${@}"
225	```
226	
227	The exec line carries no launcher token — it is identical, character for character, across every tabtarget. All per-tabtarget variation lives in the `BURD_*` block between the shebang and the exec: which launcher (`BURD_LAUNCHER`, always the first config line), and any dispatch flags (`BURD_NO_LOG`, `BURD_INTERACTIVE`) that follow.
228	
229	The trampoline has exactly two responsibilities:
230	
231	1. **Resolve the moorings location.** The consumer's configuration tree (launchers, regimes) lives under a moorings directory whose path the trampoline holds as a single hardcoded literal. This is the single point of customization at the tabtarget layer — the parallel to BURC at the CLI layer. Move the moorings, edit one line; every tabtarget follows.
232	2. **Normalize cwd to repo root.** The trampoline `cd`s to the repo root before dispatching, so every workbench starts from a deterministic directory regardless of where the user invoked the tabtarget. Downstream code relies on a known cwd rather than reconstructing one.
233	
234	**The launcher name.** `BURD_LAUNCHER` is a bare launcher basename — `launcher.<id>_workbench.sh`. Every launcher co-locates in the moorings launcher directory (`rbml_launchers/`), so the trampoline resolves the basename directly against that one directory and exec's it, forwarding the tabtarget basename and user args unchanged. `BURD_LAUNCHER` is not a private positional convention: it is a regime variable — declared in `burd_regime`, allowlisted in `bud_dispatch` — so the launcher identity travels the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value, and flows downstream unchanged.
235	
236	Why name the launcher in a config variable rather than pass it as a positional dispatch token? **Because the byte-identical exec line is load-bearing.** When the only thing that varies between two tabtargets is their `BURD_*` block, a tabtarget is purely declarative — it states *which launcher and which flags*, and holds no dispatch logic that can drift from the trampoline's expectations. The launcher identity rides the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value rather than a parallel argument slot, and the exec collapses to a true constant the qualifier checks against a single literal.
237	
238	This indirection is the tabtarget-layer instance of CLI as Module Gateway: a tabtarget is external code, and it reaches module functionality only through a launcher the trampoline resolves — never by sourcing or kindling directly. The launcher → dispatch chain it hands off to is what provides the Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables described next.
239	
240	### Dispatch-Provided Directory Variables
241	
242	Three BURD variables carry directory paths across the exec boundary into CLI processes. These are derived from BURC configuration and exported by bud_dispatch.sh during zbud_setup():
243	
244	| Variable | Source | Value |
245	|----------|--------|-------|
246	| `BURD_TOOLS_DIR` | `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | Project tools root directory |
247	| `BURD_BUK_DIR` | Derived from `BURC_TOOLS_DIR` | BUK subdirectory (`$BURC_TOOLS_DIR/buk`) |
248	| `BURD_TABTARGET_DIR` | `BURC_TABTARGET_DIR` | Tabtarget directory |
249	
250	These variables are available in exec'd CLI processes without requiring the CLI to re-source BURC. CLIs that need stable paths to tools or utilities should use these variables rather than reconstructing paths from `BASH_SOURCE`.
251	
252	### Template 2: CLI Entry Point
253	
254	BUD variables are provided by dispatch. The CLI header sources only `buc_command.sh`; all other dependencies are sourced inside the furnish function. This enables doc-mode help display without runtime env vars — furnish gates after `buc_doc_env` calls before any sourcing that might fail.
255	
256	```bash
257	# «shebang»
258	# «copyright»
259	
260	set -euo pipefail
261	
262	source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buc_command.sh"
263	
264	z«prefix»_furnish() {
265	
266	  # Document only the BUD variables actually used
267	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
268	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
269	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module specific configuration file"
270	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
271	
272	  # Source all dependencies
273	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh"
274	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/«prefix»_«name».sh"
275	
276	  # Source regime files, if any
277	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime file"
278	
279	  z«prefix»_kindle
280	  z«prefix»_enforce  # Regime archetype: ironclad gate after kindle (omit if no regime)
281	}
282	
283	buc_execute «prefix»_ "«module_description_phrase»" z«prefix»_furnish "$@"
284	
285	# eof
286	```
287	
288	### Differential Furnish (conditional dependency sourcing)
289	
290	The furnish function receives the **command name** as `$1`, enabling conditional sourcing based on which command will execute. This allows heavy dependencies (e.g., GCP client libraries, OAuth flows) to be loaded only for commands that need them.
291	
292	**Pattern**: Light dependencies always loaded; heavy dependencies conditionally sourced per command.
293	
294	```bash
295	z«prefix»_furnish() {
296	  local z_command="${1:-}"
297	
298	  # Document environment variables
299	  buc_doc_env "BURD_BUK_DIR          " "BUK module directory (dispatch-provided)"
300	  buc_doc_env "BURD_TOOLS_DIR        " "Project tools root directory (dispatch-provided)"
301	  buc_doc_env "«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE " "Module configuration file"
302	  buc_doc_env_done || return 0
303	
304	  # Always source light dependencies
305	  source "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source regime"
306	  source "${BURD_BUK_DIR}/buv_validation.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source validation"
307	
308	  # Conditionally source heavy dependencies based on command
309	  case "${z_command}" in
310	    «prefix»_survey|«prefix»_audit)
311	      # These commands need GCP and OAuth infrastructure
312	      source "${BURD_TOOLS_DIR}/some/heavy_dep.sh" || buc_die "Failed to source heavy deps"
313	      zheavy_kindle
314	      ;;
315	    «prefix»_validate|«prefix»_render)
316	      # These commands work with config only
317	      ;;
318	  esac
319	
320	  z«prefix»_kindle
321	}
322	```
323	
324	**Key points:**
325	- The furnish function receives the command name (e.g., "«prefix»_validate") as `$1`
326	- Existing furnish functions that don't use `$1` are unaffected (extra arg ignored in bash)
327	- Light, always-needed dependencies are sourced unconditionally
328	- Heavy dependencies are sourced only for the commands that need them (avoiding startup overhead)
329	- The `case` statement can be omitted if a module has no conditional dependencies
330	
331	**Benefits:**
332	- Faster startup for lightweight commands (validate, render, list)
333	- Heavy infrastructure (GCP SDKs, OAuth flows) loaded only when actually needed
334	- Reduced memory footprint for simple operations
335	- Clear visibility of which commands depend on what infrastructure
336	
337	### Template 3: Implementation Module
338	
339	```bash
340	# «shebang»
341	# «copyright»
342	
343	set -euo pipefail
344	
345	# Multiple inclusion detection
346	test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
347	Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1
348	
349	# Tinder constants (pure string literals or tinder-on-tinder composition — available at source time)
350	# «PREFIX»_some_path="fixed-filename.env"
351	# «PREFIX»_some_prefix="prefix_"
352	
353	######################################################################
354	# Internal Functions (z«prefix»_*)
355	
356	z«prefix»_kindle() {
357	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
358	
359	  # Validate only the BURD variables actually used
360	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_TEMP_DIR}"   # If using temp files
361	  buv_dir_exists "${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}"  # If producing outputs
362	  test -n "${BURD_NOW_STAMP:-}" || buc_die "BURD_NOW_STAMP is unset"  # If using timestamps
363	
364	  # Validate module specific environment
365	  buv_file_exists "${«PREFIX»_REGIME_FILE}"  # If present
366	
367	  # Source credentials into environment only - never write to disk
368	  source "${REGIME_CRED_FILE}" || buc_die "Failed to source credentials"
369	
370	  # Kindle constant (public — safe to read from other modules; never a secret)
371	  «PREFIX»_PUBLIC_SELECTED_NAME="some-specific-name"
372	
373	  # Kindle constants (internal)
374	  Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_temp.txt"  # Non-secret data only
375	  Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_result.json"
376	  Z«PREFIX»_CONFIG_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_config_"     # rest of file name appended on use
377	  Z«PREFIX»_MANIFEST_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_«step_number»_manifest_"   # rest of file name appended on use
378	
379	  # Temporary files (if using BURD_OUTPUT_DIR)
380	  Z«PREFIX»_OUTPUT_RESULT="${BURD_OUTPUT_DIR}/result.txt"
381	
382	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
383	}
384	
385	z«prefix»_sentinel() {
386	  test "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module «prefix» not kindled - call z«prefix»_kindle first"
387	}
388	
389	# Helper functions follow
390	
391	######################################################################
392	# External Functions («prefix»_*)
393	
394	«prefix»_«command»() {
395	  z«prefix»_sentinel
396	
397	  local z_param1="${1:-}"
398	  local z_param2="${2:-}"
399	
400	  # Documentation block
401	  buc_doc_brief "«one_line_description»"
402	  buc_doc_param "«required_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
403	  buc_doc_oparm "«optional_arg»" "«one_line_description»"
404	  buc_doc_shown || return 0
405	
406	  # Implementation
407	  test -n "${z_param1}" || buc_die "Parameter 'param1' is required"
408	
409	  buc_step "«user_visible_milestone»"
410	  buc_log_args "«detailed_progress_information»"
411	  [command] || buc_die "«failure_explanation»"
412	
413	  buc_success "«success_message»"
414	}
415	```
416	
417	**Kindle constant — anti-pattern:**
418	
419	```bash
420	# ❌ Kindle constant defined outside kindle
421	z«prefix»_setup() {
422	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"   # VIOLATION: must be in kindle
423	}
424	
425	# ✅ Kindle constants defined in kindle, used elsewhere
426	z«prefix»_kindle() {
427	  Z«PREFIX»_CACHED_PATH="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/cache"
428	  Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
429	}
430	```
431	
432	**Tinder vs kindle constant — choosing correctly:**
433	
434	```bash
435	# ✅ Tinder constant: pure string, available at source time
436	«PREFIX»_config_file="config.env"        # lower_snake name — no kindle needed
437	
438	# ✅ Tinder-on-tinder composition: expansion of earlier tinder constant
439	«PREFIX»_role_director="director"
440	«PREFIX»_fact_ext_director="ext-${«PREFIX»_role_director}"  # resolves at source time
441	
442	# ✅ Kindle constant: depends on runtime state, requires kindle
443	«PREFIX»_CONFIG_DIR="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # SCREAMING name — kindle required
444	
445	# ❌ Wrong: runtime variable expansion in tinder position
446	«PREFIX»_config_dir="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/config"  # VIOLATION: BURD_TEMP_DIR is runtime — must be kindle
447	
448	# ❌ Wrong: computation in tinder position
449	«PREFIX»_color_red=$(z«prefix»_color '1;31')   # VIOLATION: $() — must be in kindle
450	
451	# ❌ Wrong: expansion of kindle constant in tinder position
452	«PREFIX»_derived="${«PREFIX»_KINDLED_VAR}/sub"  # VIOLATION: KINDLED_VAR available only after kindle
453	```
454	
455	## Variable Handling (General Rules)
456	
457	### Expansion Requirements
458	
459	**Always use braced, quoted expansion:**
460	```bash
461	"${z_var}"              # Required pattern - prevents word splitting and glob expansion
462	"${Z_MODULE_VAR}"       # Module variables from kindle
463	"${1:-}"                # Parameters with defaults
464	```
465	
466	**Never use:**
467	```bash
468	$var                    # Unquoted - word splitting risk
469	"$var"                  # Unbraced - boundary ambiguity
470	${var}                  # Unquoted - glob expansion risk
471	```
472	
473	**No tilde in quoted strings** — tilde expansion is suppressed inside quotes:
474	```bash
475	# ❌ Tilde does not expand in quotes
476	"~/Downloads/file.txt"          # Literal ~ character, not home directory
477	
478	# ✅ Use $HOME instead
479	"${HOME}/Downloads/file.txt"    # Expands correctly
480	```
481	
482	**Quote inside parameter expansion operators:**
483	```bash
484	# ✅ Inner expansion quoted separately — matches literally
485	"${z_path%"${z_suffix}"}"       # Strip suffix
486	"${z_path#"${z_prefix}"}"       # Strip prefix
487	
488	# ❌ Inner expansion unquoted — matches as a glob pattern
489	"${z_path%${z_suffix}}"         # z_suffix with * or ? chars matches as glob!
490	"${z_path#${z_prefix}}"         # Same risk
491	```
492	
493	When using `%`, `%%`, `#`, `##` operators with a variable as the pattern, the inner expansion must be quoted separately. Without inner quotes, the pattern is glob-expanded — a suffix containing `*` or `?` silently matches more than intended.
494	
495	### Assignment Patterns
496	
497	```bash
498	# Direct assignment
499	local z_message="Processing item"
500	
501	# ❌ NEVER declare multiple variables on one line
502	local z_name z_value z_result  # Hidden initialization failures!
503	
504	# ✅ ALWAYS one declaration per line with explicit initialization
505	local z_name=""
506	local z_value="default"
507	local z_result=0
508	
509	# ❌ NEVER use local -i — silently coerces non-integer values to 0
510	local -i z_count="${z_input}"  # "abc" becomes 0 — violates no-silent-failures!
511	
512	# ✅ Use plain local with explicit validation
513	local z_count="${z_input}"
514	test "${z_count}" -ge 0 2>/dev/null || buc_die "z_count must be non-negative integer, got: ${z_count}"
515	
516	# ✅ Two-line pattern for captured values (explicit and debuggable)
517	local z_token
518	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
519	
520	# ❌ NEVER combine declaration with capture
521	local z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture)  # Hides exit status!
522	
523	# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
524	local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
525	test -n "${z_content}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}"
526	
527	# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
528	# ❌ local z_content
529	# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
530	
531	# Use Capture functions for secrets that must never touch disk
532	local z_token
533	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
534	
535	# Temp files INSTEAD of command substitution holds intermediate results
536	echo "${z_result}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_RESULT_FILE}"
537	complex_command > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
538	process < "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP1}" > "${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
539	local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
540	test -n "${z_result}" || buc_die "Failed to read or empty: ${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}"
541	```
542	
543	### Prefer Bash Builtins Over External Tools
544	
545	```bash
546	# ✅ String manipulation with parameter expansion
547	"${z_path##*/}"                    # Instead of: basename
548	"${z_path%/*}"                     # Instead of: dirname
549	"${z_string//old/new}"             # Instead of: sed 's/old/new/g'
550	"${z_string#prefix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/^prefix//'
551	"${z_string%suffix}"               # Instead of: sed 's/suffix$//'
552	
553	# ✅ Reading lines with 'read' — guard handles missing trailing newline
554	while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
555	  echo "Processing: ${z_line}" || buc_die "Failed to echo line"
556	done < "${Z_MODULE_INPUT_FILE}"
557	
558	# ✅ Arithmetic with $((...))
559	z_result=$((z_value * 2 + 10))    # Instead of: expr or bc
560	```
561	
562	### Temp Files Instead of Command Substitution
563	
564	All external command output capture uses temp files. The three prohibited `$()` patterns are: `local z_var=$(cmd)` (local swallows exit status), pipelines inside `$()` (hidden intermediate failures), and unguarded `$()` (silent failure). The only `$()` permitted on functions is for `_capture` (secrets that must not touch disk) and `_recite` (read-only roll access) — both with explicit `|| buc_die` or `|| return 1`. Bash introspection (`compgen`, `declare -F`, `mktemp`) is also permitted in `$()` as no file-based alternative exists.
565	
566	These rules govern **command substitution** — `$(command)`, which spawns a subshell, runs a command, and can fail. They do not apply to `$(<file)`, which despite sharing the `$()` syntax is a bash **builtin file redirect**: no subshell, no command, nothing to fail. `$(<file)` needs no `|| buc_die` guard — it takes content validation (`test -n` on the result) instead, since a successful read of an empty or missing file still yields the empty string.
567	
568	```bash
569	# ❌ Anti-pattern: nested command substitution
570	z_result=$(echo $(complex_command) | process)  # Hidden failures!
571	
572	# ❌ Anti-pattern: unguarded command substitution
573	z_timestamp=$(date +%s)  # Failure is silent!
574	
575	# ✅ Correct: temp files make failures visible afterward
576	complex_command > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Complex command failed"
577	process < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "Process failed"
578	read -r z_result < "${Z_MODULE_TEMP2}" || buc_die "No result"
579	
580	# ✅ Correct: single external command via temp file
581	date +%s > "${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}" || buc_die "Failed to get timestamp"
582	local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
583	test -n "${z_timestamp}" || buc_die "Empty timestamp"
584	
585	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: Secrets use _capture functions, never temp files
586	# ⚠️ EXCEPTION: _recite functions return via stdout (read-only roll access)
587	```
588	
589	**Temp file lifecycle**: Temp files under `BURD_TEMP_DIR` are **preserved after execution** for forensic debugging. Never delete temp files in module code — their persistence is intentional. Cleanup is handled by infrastructure outside BCG's scope.
590	
591	### Stderr Capture — Never Suppress
592	
593	Never use `2>/dev/null` on external commands. Redirect stderr to a temp file so forensic evidence is preserved when commands fail. Include the stderr file path in the `buc_die` message so the user can inspect it.
594	
595	```bash
596	# ❌ Anti-pattern: stderr suppressed — failure cause invisible
597	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>/dev/null \
598	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url}"
599	
600	# ✅ Correct: stderr captured to temp file, referenced in die message
601	curl -sS "${z_url}" -o "${z_output_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
602	  || buc_die "Failed to fetch ${z_url} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
603	```
604	
605	**Stderr file naming**: Use a kindle `_PREFIX` constant (grouped with other temp file prefixes for collision visibility) and append a discriminator at the usage site.
606	
607	**In loops**, use an auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness rather than trusting loop data values to be unique:
608	
609	```bash
610	# In kindle — grouped with sibling temp file prefixes
611	Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_«command»_"
612	
613	# In command function — integer discriminator ensures uniqueness
614	local z_index=0
615	while IFS='|' read -r z_varname z_image z_tag || test -n "${z_varname}"; do
616	  local z_manifest_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
617	  local z_stderr_file="${Z«PREFIX»_«COMMAND»_PREFIX}${z_index}_inspect_stderr.txt"
618	
619	  docker manifest inspect "${z_image}:${z_tag}" \
620	    > "${z_manifest_file}" 2>"${z_stderr_file}" \
621	    || buc_die "Failed to inspect ${z_image}:${z_tag} — see ${z_stderr_file}"
622	
623	  z_index=$((z_index + 1))
624	done
625	```
626	
627	### String Usage
628	
629	```bash
630	# Single-line HERE-string allowed for piping variables
631	jq '.field' <<<"${z_json_data}"
632	grep "pattern" <<<"${z_content}"
633	
634	# Multi-line HERE-strings and HERE-docs prohibited
635	```
636	
637	### Readonly Patterns
638	
639	Readonly enforcement makes mutation bugs loud at the point of violation rather than silent cascades downstream. Three patterns at different scopes:
640	
641	#### `local -r` — Default for Local Variables
642	
643	Use `local -r` for every local variable that is assigned once and never modified. This is the majority of locals in a typical BCG function.
644	
645	```bash
646	# ✅ Default: readonly locals
647	local -r z_filepath="${1:-}"
648	local -r z_varname="${z_buv_varname_roll[$z_idx]}"
649	local -r z_full_ref="${z_image}@${z_digest}"
650	local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
651	```
652	
653	**Four exceptions** where `local` (mutable) is required:
654	
655	```bash
656	# Exception 1: Loop counters
657	local z_index=0
658	z_index=$((z_index + 1))
659	
660	# Exception 2: Per-iteration synthesized variables (reassigned each pass)
661	local z_manifest_file=""
662	for z_spec in "${z_specs[@]}"; do
663	  z_manifest_file="${Z_PREFIX}${z_index}_manifest.json"
664	  ...
665	done
666	
667	# Exception 3: Two-line capture pattern (readonly would lock empty value)
668	local z_token
669	z_token=$(zauth_get_token_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture token"
670	
671	# Exception 4: Accumulators and state-tracking flags
672	local z_any_failed=0
673	local z_current_group=""
674	```
675	
676	**Why not `local -i`?** `local -i` silently coerces non-integers to 0 — it hides bugs. `local -r` does the opposite — it surfaces mutation bugs loudly. Same flag syntax, opposite safety properties.
677	
678	#### `readonly` — Every Kindle Constant
679	
680	Every variable assigned in a kindle function is a constant. Apply `readonly` to every assignment — whether the value is a literal, computed from other variables, or composed from prior assignments:
681	
682	```bash
683	z«prefix»_kindle() {
684	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
685	
686	  # Simple kindle constants (literal values, but defined in kindle for readonly enforcement)
687	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_API_VERSION="v2"
688	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_MAX_RETRIES=3
689	
690	  # Computed from already-set values
691	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
692	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
693	
694	  # Composed from prior kindle assignments
695	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_ACCEPT_TYPES="${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_A},${Z«PREFIX»_TYPE_B}"
696	
697	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
698	}
699	```
700	
701	If a kindle function uses intermediate variables to build up a result (e.g., composing array elements), the final assignment is still `readonly`. Intermediates that are truly local to the build-up should use `local -r`.
702	
703	**The KINDLED sentinel** is a kindle constant — it gets `readonly` like every other kindle assignment. The `test -z` guard at the top of kindle fires before any reassignment attempt, so `readonly` is belt-and-suspenders: the guard gives a friendly error message, `readonly` is the backstop.
704	
705	#### Mutable Kindle State
706	
707	Some kindle functions initialize mutable state alongside constants — counters, accumulators, registry rolls, and builder state that are modified after kindle returns. These variables **must not** be `readonly`, but they must be visually distinguishable from kindle constants.
708	
709	**Convention**: Mutable kindle state uses **lowercase** `z_«prefix»_name`. Constants use **UPPERCASE** `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`. Case alone signals mutability.
710	
711	```bash
712	z«prefix»_kindle() {
713	  test -z "${Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module «prefix» already kindled"
714	
715	  # ✅ Kindle constants — UPPERCASE, readonly
716	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_PREFIX="${BURD_TEMP_DIR}/«prefix»_"
717	
718	  # ✅ Mutable kindle state — lowercase, NO readonly
719	  z_«prefix»_file_index=0              # counter incremented post-kindle
720	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()              # registry populated by enroll calls
721	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""           # builder state set during enrollment
722	
723	  readonly Z«PREFIX»_KINDLED=1
724	}
725	```
726	
727	**Rules**:
728	- Mutable kindle state follows the same `z_` internal prefix as roll arrays
729	- Never apply `readonly` to mutable kindle state
730	- If a variable is initialized in kindle and never mutated afterward, it is a constant — use `readonly Z«PREFIX»_NAME`
731	- The `_roll` suffix is conventional for parallel-array registries but not required for all mutable state (counters, flags, builder state use descriptive names)
732	
733	**Test reset**: When tests need fresh enrollment state, provide an internal reset function that clears mutable kindle state without touching `KINDLED`. Re-kindling a module is never correct — the sentinel is `readonly`.
734	
735	```bash
736	# ✅ Test support: reset mutable state, module stays kindled
737	z«prefix»_reset_enrollment() {
738	  z«prefix»_sentinel
739	  z_«prefix»_name_roll=()
740	  z_«prefix»_current_scope=""
741	  # ... clear all mutable kindle state ...
742	}
743	```
744	
745	**The re-source trap** — `readonly` belongs in code, never in sourceable `.env` files:
746	```bash
747	# ❌ NEVER in sourceable .env files
748	# If a .env file contains `readonly FOO=bar`, sourcing it a second time
749	# (e.g., regime reload, test re-initialization) dies with:
750	#   bash: FOO: readonly variable
751	```
752	
753	#### `readonly VAR` — Lock After Enforce
754	
755	For regime variables loaded from `.env` files, lock them *after* validation succeeds. This is the regime archetype's lock step (see Regime Module Archetype above):
756	
757	```bash
758	z«prefix»_enforce() {
759	  z«prefix»_sentinel
760	  buv_vet «SCOPE»
761	  # ... custom format checks ...
762	}
763	
764	# In the lock step (called after enforce succeeds):
765	# Lock all enrolled variables — any downstream mutation is now a loud error
766	readonly REGI_FIELD_ONE REGI_FIELD_TWO REGI_FIELD_THREE
767	
768	# Derived state built from validated values:
769	Z«PREFIX»_DOCKER_ENV=("-e" "REGI_FIELD_ONE=${REGI_FIELD_ONE}")
770	```
771	
772	**Ordering matters**: Build derived state (docker env arrays, rollup strings) *after* enforce but *before* or *during* the lock call. Once variables are readonly, derived state captures validated, immutable values.
773	
774	## Special Function Definitions
775	
776	### Special Function Patterns
777	
778	This table defines scope: «prefix»_* is public, z«prefix»_* is internal.
779	
780	| Type      | Pattern                        | First Line           | Returns                      | buc_step                  | Contract                            |
781	|-----------|--------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------|
782	| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
783	| Capture   | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_capture`   | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout once at end or exit 1 | No (use buc_log_«source») | Clean error handling, single return |
784	| Enroll    | `[z]«prefix»_[«scope»_]enroll` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | `z_«funcname»_«retval»` vars | No (use buc_log_«source») | Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) in kindle only; returns via variables |
785	| Recite    | `[z]«prefix»_«what»_recite`    | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | stdout or exit 1             | No (use buc_log_«source») | Read-only access to rolls; never mutates |
786	| Fixture baste | `z«tb»_«name»_baste`         | —                   | — (side effects only)        | Optional                  | Fixture subshell; kindle, source, configure |
787	| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
788	| Yawp      | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_yawp`     | `z«module»_sentinel` | `z_«module»_«group»` var    | No                        | Pure assignment, cannot fail, no output |
789	
790	---
791	
792	#### Capture Functions (controlled command substitution)
793	
794	**Purpose: Return a single processed value through stdout with clean error handling.**
795	
796	Capture functions encapsulate command substitution to ensure predictable error handling and single-point-of-return semantics.
797	
798	```bash
799	z«prefix»_«name»_capture() {
800	  z«prefix»_sentinel
801	
802	  # Process and validate internally
803	  local z_result
804	  z_result=$(some_command | process) || return 1
805	
806	  # Validate without buc_die
807	  test -n "${z_result}" || return 1
808	
809	  # Single output at end
810	  echo "${z_result}"
811	}
812	
813	# Caller uses two-line pattern
814	local z_value
815	z_value=$(z«prefix»_«name»_capture) || buc_die "Failed to capture value"
816	```
817	
818	**Use capture functions for:**
819	- Secrets that must never touch disk
820	- Values requiring command substitution or pipeline processing
821	- Any computed value where clean error handling matters
822	
823	**Contract:**
824	- Returns stdout once at end or exits with status 1
825	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
826	- No side effects beyond the returned value
827	- May source credential files when necessary for security
828	- May use `buc_log_«source»` for forensic trail (writes to transcript file only)
829	- Never writes secrets to disk
830	- Never writes to stderr
831	
832	#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
833	
834	Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
835	
836	```bash
837	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
838	  z«prefix»_sentinel
839	
840	  local z_file="${1:-}"
841	
842	  # Return status only - no output, no dies
843	  test -f "${z_file}" || return 1
844	  grep -q "required_pattern" "${z_file}" || return 1
845	
846	  return 0
847	}
848	
849	# Caller uses in conditionals
850	z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
851	```
852	
853	#### Enroll Functions (kindle-only registry population)
854	
855	**Purpose**: Populate parallel-array registries (rolls) during module initialization. Enroll functions solve the subshell problem: when a function both mutates shared state and returns a value, calling it in `$()` loses the mutations.
856	
857	**The parallel-array registry pattern (rolls)**:
858	- Kindle initializes N empty arrays (rolls) of the same length
859	- Enroll validates inputs and appends atomically to all rolls
860	- Recite functions (see below) provide read-only access after kindle completes
861	- Variable naming: `z_«prefix»_«name»_roll` for all roll arrays (internal state, initialized in kindle)
862	
863	**Example** (using synthetic notation — «reg» owns the rolls, «con» consumes them):
864	
865	```bash
866	# Module «reg» — owns the rolls
867	z«reg»_kindle() {
868	  test -z "${Z«REG»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
869	
870	  z_«reg»_name_roll=()
871	  z_«reg»_target_roll=()
872	  z_«reg»_handler_roll=()
873	
874	  Z«REG»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last — sentinel guards all subsequent calls
875	}
876	
877	# Enroll function — called by consuming module's kindle, NOT «reg»'s own kindle
878	«reg»_enroll() {
879	  z«reg»_sentinel   # Safe: «reg» is already kindled when consumer calls this
880	
881	  local z_name="${1:-}"
882	  local z_target="${2:-}"
883	  local z_handler="${3:-}"
884	  test -n "${z_name}" || buc_die "enroll: name required"
885	
886	  # Registration-time validation
887	  declare -F "${z_handler}" >/dev/null || buc_die "enroll: handler not found: ${z_handler}"
888	
889	  z_«reg»_name_roll+=("${z_name}")
890	  z_«reg»_target_roll+=("${z_target}")
891	  z_«reg»_handler_roll+=("${z_handler}")
892	
893	  # Return via variable — NOT echo
894	  z_«reg»_enroll_name="${z_name}"
895	}
896	
897	# Module «con» — consumes the registry
898	z«con»_kindle() {
899	  test -z "${Z«CON»_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "already kindled"
900	
901	  # «reg» must be kindled first (rolls initialized, sentinel passes)
902	  «reg»_enroll "alpha" "/path/alpha" "handle_alpha"
903	  Z«CON»_DEFAULT="${z_«reg»_enroll_name}"
904	
905	  «reg»_enroll "bravo" "/path/bravo" "handle_bravo"
906	
907	  Z«CON»_KINDLED=1   # MUST be last
908	}
909	```
910	
911	**Key constraints**:
912	- Enroll functions may ONLY be called within kindle
913	- Rolls are initialized in kindle, populated by enroll in kindle, then immutable after kindle completes
914	- Enroll functions work exclusively with rolls (parallel arrays) — no other shared state mutation
915	- Registration-time validation: check invariants at enroll time, not at recite/execution time
916	- Return values use `z_«funcname»_«retval»` convention (function name embedded verbatim for traceability)
917	- Must NOT be called inside `$()` — side effects would be lost
918	- May use `buc_die` internally (unlike `_capture`)
919	
920	**Two-level registries**: When entities have parent-child relationships (e.g., groups containing items), use two flat registries with a foreign-key column rather than per-parent dynamic arrays. This avoids `eval` and stays bash 3.2 safe.
921	
922	**Contract summary**:
923	- Mutates rolls (parallel arrays) only
924	- Returns value(s) via `z_«funcname»_«retval»` variables (NOT echo)
925	- Called exclusively within kindle
926	- Should rarely be used — only when a function must both mutate and return
927	
928	#### Recite Functions (read-only roll access)
929	
930	**Purpose: Provide read-only access to roll arrays populated by enroll functions.**
931	
932	Recite functions are the only access path to rolls after kindle completes. They must never mutate roll arrays.
933	
934	```bash
935	«prefix»_«what»_recite() {
936	  z«prefix»_sentinel
937	
938	  local z_key="${1:-}"
939	  test -n "${z_key}" || return 1
940	
941	  local z_i=0
942	  for z_i in "${!z_«prefix»_name_roll[@]}"; do
943	    test "${z_«prefix»_name_roll[$z_i]}" = "${z_key}" || continue
944	    echo "${z_«prefix»_target_roll[$z_i]}" || return 1
945	    return 0
946	  done
947	  return 1
948	}
949	
950	# Caller uses two-line capture pattern
951	local z_target
952	z_target=$(«prefix»_target_recite "alpha") || buc_die "not found"
953	```
954	
955	**Contract:**
956	- Read-only: MUST NOT mutate roll arrays or any shared state
957	- Returns via echo (like capture functions) — safe to call in `$()`
958	- Returns 1 on not-found (like `_capture`) — never uses `buc_die` internally
959	- Caller decides error handling: `|| buc_die` or conditional
960	- Usable anywhere after kindle completes (not restricted to kindle)
961	
962	#### Yawp Functions (pure-computation group return)
963	
964	**Purpose: Produce a value into a shared group return variable via pure bash assignment. No subshells, no stdout, no stderr.**
965	
966	Named for Whitman's "barbaric yawp" — a loud, unmistakeable declaration into a known place. Yawp functions solve the high-frequency value-production problem: when dozens of calls per function each need a computed string, `$()` subshell overhead is disproportionate to the work being done.
967	
968	```bash
969	# «prefix»_«name»_yawp arg1 [arg2] — what it stamps
970	# → local -r z_name="${z_«module»_«group»}"
971	«prefix»_«name»_yawp() {
972	  z«module»_sentinel
973	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_PREFIX}${1:-}${ZMOD_SUFFIX}"
974	}
975	```
976	
977	The two-line comment is the per-function template: signature on line 1, mandatory capture pattern on line 2. The `→` arrow is a visual reminder that the caller must capture on the same line.
978	
979	**Return variable**: `z_«module»_«group»` — mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle. One per function group. All `_yawp` functions in a group share the same return variable. The value is valid until the next `_yawp` call in the same group.
980	
981	```bash
982	# In kindle — initialize mutable kindle state for yawp group
983	z_«module»_«group»=""
984	
985	# In function — pure assignment, no process spawning
986	«prefix»_cmd_yawp() {
987	  z«module»_sentinel
988	  z_«module»_«group»="${ZMOD_MARKER_CMD}${1:-}${ZMOD_MARKER_END}"
989	}
990	
991	# Caller pattern — semicolon capture on same line
992	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
993	«prefix»_ui_yawp "Save";         local -r z_ui="${z_«module»_«group»}"
994	```
995	
996	**Contract:**
997	- Sets `z_«module»_«group»` (mutable kindle state, initialized in kindle)
998	- **Caller MUST capture to `local -r` on the same line** — semicolon-separated, value is destroyed by the next `_yawp` call in the same group
999	- Pure bash builtins only — no external commands, no process spawning
1000	- Cannot fail — no `|| buc_die` needed at call site
1001	- Never uses `buc_die` internally
1002	- No stdout, no stderr
1003	- First line: sentinel check
1004	
1005	**Capture discipline — the return variable is a transient slot, not a durable value:**
1006	
1007	```bash
1008	# CORRECT — semicolon capture on same line, compose from captured locals
1009	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status";  local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
1010	«prefix»_tt_yawp "«colophon»";      local -r z_tt="${z_«module»_«group»}"
1011	buh_line "Run ${z_cmd} to validate: ${z_tt}"
1012	
1013	# AVOID — two-line form works but doesn't enforce discipline
1014	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
1015	local -r z_cmd="${z_«module»_«group»}"
1016	# ↑ Nothing prevents inserting code between yawp and capture.
1017	# The semicolon form makes interleaving structurally impossible.
1018	
1019	# WRONG — z_«group» used directly in output (fragile, unreadable)
1020	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
1021	buh_line "Run ${z_«module»_«group»}"
1022	
1023	# WRONG — two yawps before capture (first value lost)
1024	«prefix»_cmd_yawp "git status"
1025	«prefix»_tt_yawp "«colophon»"
1026	buh_line "${z_«module»_«group»}"  # only has tt, cmd is gone
1027	```
1028	
1029	The semicolon makes the capture discipline visually self-enforcing — you cannot accidentally insert code between the yawp and its capture. The two-line form invited interleaving mistakes; the one-line form eliminates them structurally.
1030	
1031	The one-yawp-one-capture rhythm is the invariant. Pre-captured constants (e.g., `RBYC_*` from `zrbyc_kindle`) are safe to interpolate directly because they were captured at kindle time — the discipline applies at the yawp call site, not at the interpolation site.
1032	
1033	**Use yawp functions for:**
1034	- Pure string assembly (concatenation, marker stamping)
1035	- Value production where `$()` subshell overhead is unnecessary
